TENS has no pharmacological side effects, can be used safely with other treatments, and devices are available over the counter. It is endorsed by NICE as an option for dysmenorrhea.

what is the evidence for acupuncture in: Acupuncture (the insertion of thin needles at specific body points) has been studied for several menstrual conditions with varying evidence quality. For dysmenorrhea: a 2016 Cochrane systematic review (47 RCTs) found acupuncture more effective than NSAIDs and no-treatment controls for reducing period pain, with effect sizes comparable to pharmacological treatment, though trial quality was generally low. For PCOS: small trials suggest acupuncture may modulate LH and testosterone levels and improve menstrual regularity, but evidence is insufficient for clinical recommendations. For IVF outcomes: a 2008 large UK trial (Domar et al.) found no benefit of acupuncture around embryo transfer on IVF outcomes. For endometriosis pain: limited pilot data suggests benefit but no large RCTs exist. Overall, acupuncture appears safe and may provide meaningful benefit for dysmenorrhea as an adjunct or alternative for those preferring non-pharmacological management.

what is seed cycling: Seed cycling is a nutritional approach that involves consuming specific seeds during different phases of the menstrual cycle, purportedly to support hormonal balance. In the follicular phase (day 1 to 14), proponents recommend flaxseeds and pumpkin seeds. In the luteal phase (day 15 to 28), sesame seeds and sunflower seeds. The proposed mechanisms include: flaxseeds containing lignans (phytoestrogens that may modulate estrogen activity) and high omega-3 content; pumpkin seeds providing zinc (which supports progesterone synthesis in the luteal phase); sesame seeds providing lignans and selenium; sunflower seeds providing vitamin E and selenium (supporting progesterone). While the individual seeds all contain nutritionally relevant compounds, no clinical trials have specifically tested seed cycling as a protocol. The concept has biological plausibility but lacks direct evidence. Consuming a variety of seeds throughout the cycle provides the nutritional benefits regardless of the specific phase protocol.

what is sperm health and how does male f: Approximately 40 to 50% of infertility cases involve a male factor. Sperm health is assessed by semen analysis, which measures: volume (normal ≥1.4 ml); concentration (normal ≥16 million/ml); total motility (normal ≥42%); progressive motility (normal ≥30%); morphology (normal forms ≥4% by strict Kruger criteria). A normal semen analysis does not guarantee fertility — sperm DNA fragmentation (damage to genetic material within the sperm) is not captured by standard semen analysis and is associated with reduced IVF success, higher miscarriage rates, and early embryo arrest. Lifestyle factors improving sperm health: not smoking, limiting alcohol, maintaining healthy weight, avoiding anabolic steroids and recreational drugs, avoiding hot baths/saunas (heat damages sperm production), and adequate zinc and antioxidant intake. Sperm production takes approximately 72 days (spermatogenesis cycle) — lifestyle changes take 3 months to fully improve sperm quality.

what is a semen analysis and what do the: A semen analysis is the primary investigation for male factor infertility, performed on a sample collected by masturbation after 2 to 7 days of abstinence (longer abstinence reduces motility; shorter reduces volume). WHO 2021 reference ranges for normal parameters: volume ≥1.4 ml; pH ≥7.2; total sperm number ≥39 million; concentration ≥16 million per ml; total motility ≥42%; progressive motility ≥30%; morphology ≥4% normal forms. Oligospermia is low sperm count; asthenospermia is poor motility; teratospermia is abnormal morphology. A single abnormal semen analysis should be repeated after 3 months as there is natural variation. Azoospermia (complete absence of sperm) requires referral to a urologist to distinguish obstructive (sperm produced but not reaching ejaculate — potentially treatable) from non-obstructive (failure of spermatogenesis — may require donor sperm or TESE).

what is sperm DNA fragmentation: Sperm DNA fragmentation (SDF) refers to breaks or damage in the DNA strands within sperm. Unlike morphology and motility, which assess the external characteristics of sperm, SDF assesses the integrity of the genetic material. A high SDF index (above 25 to 30% by SCSA or DFI test) is associated with reduced natural conception rates, lower IVF fertilisation rates, poor embryo development, higher miscarriage rates (particularly recurrent miscarriage), and poor ICSI outcomes. Standard semen analysis does not measure SDF — it requires a separate test. Causes of high SDF include advanced age, varicocele, elevated testicular temperature, smoking, alcohol, and oxidative stress. Lifestyle modifications (antioxidant supplementation — vitamin C, E, CoQ10; smoking cessation; avoiding heat exposure; varicocele repair) can reduce SDF. ICSI with surgical sperm retrieval (bypassing the epididymis where some SDF occurs) is used in severe cases.

what is azoospermia: Azoospermia is the complete absence of sperm in the ejaculate and affects approximately 1% of all men and 10 to 15% of infertile men. It is classified as obstructive (OA) or non-obstructive (NOA). In OA, sperm are produced normally in the testes but cannot reach the ejaculate due to blockage — causes include vasectomy, prior infections (epididymitis, chlamydia), congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD, associated with CFTR gene mutations — cystic fibrosis carrier status), and prior surgical trauma. In NOA, spermatogenesis itself is impaired — causes include Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), Y chromosome microdeletions, chemotherapy, testicular failure, and hormonal deficiencies. Obstructive azoospermia can often be treated by surgical sperm retrieval (PESA, TESA, TESE) combined with ICSI, achieving good pregnancy rates. Non-obstructive azoospermia requires micro-TESE (microsurgical testicular sperm extraction) to find focal areas of sperm production, with lower success rates. Donor sperm is an alternative.

what is a varicocele and does it affect: A varicocele is an abnormal dilation of the pampiniform plexus (venous plexus) in the scrotum, analogous to varicose veins. It is present in approximately 15% of all men and 35% of men with primary infertility, and up to 80% of men with secondary infertility (unexplained decline in fertility after previous successful conception). The mechanism by which varicoceles impair sperm quality involves elevated scrotal temperature (venous blood flowing in the wrong direction carries heat from the abdomen to the testis), increased oxidative stress, and reduced oxygen delivery to sperm-producing cells. Effects on semen: reduced sperm count, motility, and morphology; increased sperm DNA fragmentation. Varicocelectomy (surgical ligation or embolisation of the abnormal veins) improves semen parameters in approximately 60 to 70% of men and improves natural pregnancy rates. The decision to treat is guided by clinical grade (palpable varicoceles — Grade 1 to 3), partner's fertility status, and semen analysis results.

what is natural cycle IVF and minimal st: Natural cycle IVF retrieves the single egg that develops during a woman's natural menstrual cycle without ovarian stimulation. It is appealing for its lack of injections and OHSS risk, but success rates per cycle are significantly lower (approximately 5 to 10% per cycle) because only one egg is retrieved. Multiple cycles are typically needed. It is most suitable for women with normal ovarian reserve and low AMH/FSH who prefer to avoid stimulation, or for those for whom stimulation is medically inappropriate. Minimal stimulation IVF (mini-IVF) uses low doses of oral medications (clomiphene or letrozole) with minimal or no injectable gonadotrophins to recruit a small number of follicles (2 to 4). It reduces cost and medication burden compared to conventional IVF while retrieving more eggs than natural cycle IVF, though success rates per retrieval remain lower than conventional stimulated IVF in most centres.

what is the rate of AMH decline with age: AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone) declines continuously from peak levels around age 25 until it becomes undetectable approximately 5 years before menopause. The decline is not linear — it is gradual in the 20s and early 30s, then accelerates after age 35 to 37. Population reference ranges by age: at 25, median AMH is approximately 4 to 5 pmol/L; at 30, approximately 3 to 4 pmol/L; at 35, approximately 2 pmol/L; at 40, approximately 1 pmol/L; below 45, often <0.5 pmol/L. There is substantial individual variation — some 30-year-olds have AMH comparable to a 40-year-old. AMH reflects egg quantity but not directly egg quality. A low AMH does not mean conception is impossible — it indicates a smaller pool but does not measure whether the remaining eggs are chromosomally normal. AMH testing can inform family planning decisions by indicating whether egg quantity is declining faster than expected for age.

what is an episiotomy and the different: An episiotomy is a surgical incision into the perineum (the tissue between the vaginal opening and anus) made during the second stage of labour to facilitate delivery. It is no longer routinely recommended — a controlled spontaneous tear or mediolateral episiotomy for specific indications (instrumental delivery, imminent severe tear, fetal distress) is preferred over routine episiotomy. Perineal tears are classified by grade: First degree (superficial tear of skin only — minimal treatment needed), Second degree (skin and perineal muscles — requires suturing), Third degree (extending into the anal sphincter — 3a <50% of external sphincter, 3b >50% of external sphincter, 3c including internal sphincter — requires specialised repair in theatre), Fourth degree (through the entire sphincter complex into the rectal mucosa — requires expert surgical repair). Third and fourth degree tears (OASI — obstetric anal sphincter injuries) affect approximately 3% of vaginal births and require specialist follow-up and pelvic floor physiotherapy to address bowel and urinary function.

what is postpartum haemorrhage: Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is blood loss of 500 ml or more after vaginal delivery, or 1000 ml or more after caesarean section, within 24 hours of birth (primary PPH). Secondary PPH is abnormal bleeding between 24 hours and 12 weeks postpartum, usually from infection or retained placental tissue. PPH is a leading cause of maternal mortality globally (though rare in high-income countries with access to emergency care). Risk factors include uterine atony (failure of the uterus to contract after delivery — the most common cause, responsible for 80% of PPH), placenta praevia or accreta, prolonged labour, multiple pregnancy, large baby, and prior PPH. Prevention: active management of the third stage of labour (oxytocin given with or after delivery of the baby, controlled cord traction, uterine massage). Treatment is stepwise: oxytocin, misoprostol, tranexamic acid, surgical and interventional radiology approaches for refractory haemorrhage.

what is pelvic congestion syndrome: Pelvic congestion syndrome (PCS) is a cause of chronic pelvic pain resulting from ovarian and pelvic vein incompetence — varicosities develop within the pelvis (similar to varicose veins in the legs) because venous valves fail, causing retrograde blood flow and venous pooling. It is a common but underdiagnosed cause of chronic pelvic pain in premenopausal women (particularly those who have had multiple pregnancies). Symptoms: dull, aching pelvic pain that worsens with prolonged standing, walking, sexual intercourse (particularly after orgasm, when pelvic vasodilation is maximal), and premenstrually. It often improves on lying down. Diagnosis is by transvaginal ultrasound (dilated pelvic veins), MRI/CT venography, or laparoscopy. Treatment options include: GnRH agonists or hormonal contraception (to suppress ovarian vein filling); minimally invasive ovarian vein embolisation (coil or sclerosant injection of incompetent ovarian veins — the most effective treatment, with approximately 75% symptom improvement); and, rarely, surgical ligation.

what is pudendal neuralgia: Pudendal neuralgia is chronic pain in the distribution of the pudendal nerve — encompassing the vulva, vagina, perineum, anus, and in some people the urethra. The pudendal nerve carries sensation from all these structures and innervates the external urethral and anal sphincters. Symptoms: burning, stabbing, electric, or pins-and-needles pain in the perineal region, typically worse with sitting (which compresses the nerve at Alcock's canal), improved with standing or lying, and often worsening throughout the day. It may be triggered by childbirth (stretching/compression), cycling (compression of the nerve against the bicycle seat), pelvic surgery, or develop without identifiable cause. Diagnosis is clinical — the Nantes criteria define pudendal neuralgia. Treatment: physiotherapy (soft tissue mobilisation of the obturator internus, neural mobilisation techniques), pudendal nerve block (corticosteroid + local anaesthetic at Alcock's canal), pulsed radiofrequency of the pudendal nerve, neuromodulation (sacral nerve stimulation), and low-dose tricyclic antidepressants or pregabalin for neuropathic pain.

what is the histamine-estrogen connectio: Histamine and estrogen have a bidirectional relationship that is clinically significant for women with allergies, mast cell conditions, and hormonal disorders. Estrogen stimulates mast cells to release histamine. Conversely, histamine stimulates estrogen production from the ovaries. This creates a positive feedback loop. Elevated histamine causes: increased sensitivity to allergic triggers, worse allergy and asthma symptoms around ovulation and the late follicular phase (when estrogen peaks), cycle-phase-related worsening of urticaria (hives), headaches, digestive symptoms, and systemic symptoms in those with mast cell activation syndrome. Histamine also delays progesterone production, potentially shortening or destabilising the luteal phase. A low-histamine diet reduces the histamine load that the body's own histamine output (from estrogen stimulation) must process. Vitamin B6, DAO enzyme supplements, and diamine oxidase (the enzyme that degrades histamine) supplementation are used in clinical practice to manage the histamine-estrogen link.

what is mast cell activation syndrome an: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS) is a condition in which mast cells (immune cells found throughout the body) activate inappropriately and excessively, releasing inflammatory mediators (histamine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, tryptase) in response to triggers. MCAS disproportionately affects women and often worsens cyclically with the menstrual cycle. The estrogen-histamine positive feedback loop amplifies mast cell activation in the follicular phase around ovulation. Symptoms can include severe dysmenorrhea (prostaglandins from mast cells), heavy periods, urticaria or flushing around ovulation or menstruation, cyclical gastrointestinal symptoms, anaphylaxis-like reactions, fatigue, and brain fog that follows the cycle pattern. MCAS often overlaps with endometriosis (which is itself an inflammatory condition with mast cell involvement), PCOS, hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and POTS. Management includes a low-histamine diet, antihistamines (both H1 and H2 blockers), mast cell stabilisers (sodium cromoglycate), and hormonal approaches to stabilise the estrogen-histamine cycle.

what is hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndro: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) and hypermobility spectrum disorder (HSD) are connective tissue disorders characterised by joint hypermobility, chronic pain, and a wide range of systemic manifestations due to abnormal collagen structure. Women with hEDS commonly experience more severe menstrual symptoms. Reasons include: increased uterine and ligamentous laxity causing more intense cramping and pelvic instability; altered prostaglandin processing from abnormal connective tissue; frequent comorbidities including POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome) that worsens with the fluid shifts of menstruation; MCAS comorbidity amplifying menstrual inflammation; and pelvic floor dysfunction common in hEDS causing pelvic pain and bladder symptoms that worsen cyclically. Periods in hEDS are frequently heavier than average and more painful. Recognition of this association has improved in recent years — gynaecologists managing heavy or painful periods should enquire about hypermobility features.

what is POTS and how does it relate to t: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) is a form of dysautonomia characterised by an excessive heart rate increase (≥30 bpm in adults, ≥40 bpm in adolescents) upon standing upright from lying, causing lightheadedness, palpitations, fatigue, brain fog, and presyncope. POTS affects women approximately 5 times more than men and typically develops in the reproductive years. The menstrual cycle significantly modulates POTS symptoms. In the luteal phase, progesterone and aldosterone cause fluid redistribution, altering blood volume and autonomic regulation. Premenstrually and during menstruation, blood and fluid loss combined with prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation and inflammation dramatically worsen POTS symptoms. Increased salt and fluid intake, compression garments, and fludrocortisone (a mineralocorticoid that increases blood volume) are particularly important in managing POTS around menstruation. Many women with POTS report their worst symptoms in the perimenstrual window.

what is the Ehlers-Danlos syndrome POTS: A significant subset of patients with hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) have comorbid postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) and mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS) — sometimes called the hEDS-POTS-MCAS triad or the connective tissue disorder triumvirate. The three conditions share common pathophysiological threads: abnormal connective tissue (in hEDS) reduces vascular tone and increases venous pooling (contributing to POTS), and may alter mast cell adhesion and signalling (contributing to MCAS). Each condition amplifies the others. Women with this triad experience particularly complex menstrual and gynaecological symptom profiles — severe dysmenorrhea, heavy periods, cyclical POTS flares, and perimenstrual MCAS reactions. Recognition of this triad is increasingly important in gynaecological and multidisciplinary care, as management must address all three conditions simultaneously rather than in isolation.

what is chronic pelvic pain and the role: In chronic pelvic pain (CPP), persistent pain input from pelvic pathology (endometriosis, adenomyosis, bladder pathology, pelvic floor dysfunction) over time causes neuroplastic changes in spinal cord and brain pain processing — a phenomenon called central sensitisation. In central sensitisation, the nervous system becomes hyperreactive: normal stimuli cause pain (allodynia), pain is disproportionately severe (hyperalgesia), and pain persists after the original tissue trigger has been treated or healed. This explains why women with endometriosis may have persistent pain despite complete surgical excision, or why medical treatments that eliminate the physical pathology do not fully resolve pain. Central sensitisation is diagnosed clinically (widespread sensitivity, poor response to anti-inflammatory treatments, psychological comorbidity, spread of pain beyond the pelvis). Treatment must address the central sensitisation component alongside peripheral pathology: pain psychology (pain neuroscience education, CBT), graded activity, neuromodulatory medications (low-dose amitriptyline, pregabalin, duloxetine), and multidisciplinary pain management.

what is gonorrhoea and how does it affec: Gonorrhoea is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by the bacterium Neisseria gonorrhoeae. In women, symptoms are often absent — approximately 50% of infected women have no symptoms. When symptomatic: increased or changed vaginal discharge (yellow or green), burning with urination, pelvic pain, and bleeding between periods or after sex. If untreated, gonorrhoea ascends to cause pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), fallopian tube scarring, increased ectopic pregnancy risk, and infertility. Gonorrhoea in pregnancy can cause ophthalmia neonatorum (serious eye infection in the newborn). Diagnosis is by NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) from vaginal or cervical swab, or first-catch urine. Treatment: dual antibiotic therapy (ceftriaxone 1g IM injection is now the standard first-line treatment in the UK due to increasing antimicrobial resistance; oral azithromycin is no longer recommended as monotherapy). Sexual partners require treatment. Test of cure 2 weeks after treatment is essential.

what is mycoplasma genitalium and why do: Mycoplasma genitalium (Mg) is an emerging sexually transmitted bacterium increasingly recognised as a significant cause of urethritis (men), cervicitis, PID, and potentially adverse pregnancy outcomes. It is estimated to affect 1 to 2% of the general population. Symptoms in women: vaginal discharge, pelvic pain, intermenstrual or post-coital bleeding, and pain during sex — often indistinguishable from chlamydia. Standard STI screens do not always include Mg testing — a specific NAAT test is required. Treatment is more complex than chlamydia: doxycycline reduces bacterial load but does not eradicate Mg, which is then treated with azithromycin (in azithromycin-susceptible strains) or moxifloxacin (in resistant strains). Antibiotic resistance in Mg is increasing rapidly — resistance-guided therapy (testing for macrolide resistance mutations before prescribing) is now recommended by BASHH guidelines. Untreated or inadequately treated Mg is associated with PID and subfertility.

what is sexual health screening and how: Sexual health screening recommendations depend on risk factors. UK guidelines (BASHH/PHE) recommend annual STI testing for sexually active people under 25 and for those with new or multiple partners. A standard sexual health screen typically includes: HIV test (by blood test or point-of-care test); syphilis serology (blood test); gonorrhoea and chlamydia (by self-taken vaginal swab or first-catch urine — NAAT test); hepatitis B serology (in those at risk); and, increasingly, Mycoplasma genitalium and trichomonas testing. Swabs from additional sites (throat, rectum) are taken when relevant based on sexual history. In the UK, testing is available free of charge at NHS sexual health (GUM) clinics, GP practices, and online self-testing services. Self-taken swab testing from home (available via Royal Mail and clinic-based services) has high sensitivity and specificity and is recommended in national guidelines as equivalent to clinician-taken samples.

what is the environmental impact of disp: Disposable menstrual products have a significant environmental footprint. A single disposable pad contains the plastic equivalent of approximately 4 carrier bags. A typical menstruating person uses 11,000 to 16,000 disposable pads or tampons over their lifetime, generating approximately 200 kg of waste. Most disposable pads contain non-biodegradable plastic in the leak-proof backing, absorbent core (superabsorbent polymers), and packaging. Conventional tampons may contain non-organic cotton (pesticide-intensive crop) and non-biodegradable applicators. Their carbon footprint across their lifecycle (raw material, manufacturing, distribution, disposal) is substantial. Reusable alternatives dramatically reduce environmental impact: a menstrual cup used over 10 years has approximately 1.5% of the carbon footprint of disposable pads over the same period. Menstrual underwear and reusable cloth pads, washed and reused for several years, represent similarly low-impact alternatives. Compostable and organic disposable options reduce some impacts but still generate single-use waste.

what are the long-term health consequenc: Untreated heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) carries significant long-term health consequences. Iron deficiency anaemia is the most common and impactful: chronic blood loss depletes iron stores, reducing haemoglobin and oxygen-carrying capacity, causing fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, cognitive impairment, impaired immune function, and in severe cases palpitations and dyspnoea. Iron depletion (low ferritin with normal haemoglobin) causes symptoms before frank anaemia and is frequently missed because ferritin is not routinely checked. Chronic pain from underlying conditions (adenomyosis, fibroids, endometriosis) causing heavy bleeding goes undiagnosed and untreated. Endometrial cancer risk is elevated in those with infrequent periods from PCOS, as chronic unopposed estrogen stimulates endometrial growth — not treating the menstrual irregularity or providing progestogen to protect the endometrium leaves this risk unmitigated. Mental health and quality of life are significantly impaired by untreated heavy periods — educational and occupational disadvantage accumulate over years.

what is the menstrual cycle as a vital s: In 2015, the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) published a Committee Opinion (later reaffirmed) designating the menstrual cycle as a vital sign — alongside blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, and respiratory rate. The rationale: the menstrual cycle is a sensitive indicator of overall health status. Changes in cycle length, flow, and symptoms reflect the function of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, which integrates signals from nutritional status, metabolic health, psychological wellbeing, and physical stress. Disruption of the menstrual cycle signals systemic abnormality. Systematic documentation of menstrual patterns in clinical practice (asking about cycle length, duration, flow volume, pain, and symptoms at every relevant health encounter) enables early identification of conditions including thyroid disease, PCOS, premature ovarian insufficiency, and nutritional disorders. This framing also legitimises menstrual cycle monitoring as a clinical priority rather than an ancillary concern.

what is the connection between the menst: The immune system is regulated by sex hormones throughout the menstrual cycle. Estrogen generally has pro-inflammatory and immunostimulatory effects — it activates B cells (antibody production), T helper cells, and natural killer cells. In the follicular phase, rising estrogen amplifies immune vigilance. Progesterone in the luteal phase has immunosuppressive properties — it shifts the immune response toward a Th2 (tolerogenic, anti-inflammatory) profile to create a hospitable environment for a potential embryo. This shift explains why autoimmune conditions (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis) typically flare premenstrually (when estrogen is falling and progesterone is withdrawing) and improve during pregnancy (sustained high progesterone). Menstruation itself involves a precisely coordinated local immune response — Th1 pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, TNF-alpha, IL-6) facilitate endometrial breakdown, while anti-inflammatory mediators allow tissue repair. Disruption of this immune cycling contributes to conditions like endometriosis (where the normal pro-inflammatory menstrual response may propagate disease).

what is the role of kisspeptin neurons i: Kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus (called KNDy neurons because they co-express kisspeptin, neurokinin B, and dynorphin) are the master regulator of GnRH pulsatility. They fire in synchronised bursts that drive GnRH pulses, which in turn drive FSH and LH. In the follicular phase, neurokinin B accelerates KNDy neuron firing rate (increasing pulse frequency), driving the rising LH and FSH needed for follicle development. In the luteal phase, dynorphin slows KNDy firing (reducing pulse frequency), allowing high-frequency FSH stimulation to subside. The pre-ovulatory LH surge is triggered by kisspeptin neurons in the anteroventral periventricular nucleus (AVPV) responding to high estrogen — a positive feedback mechanism. This AVPV kisspeptin surge is the neurobiological switch that triggers ovulation. Fezolinetant (a neurokinin 3 receptor antagonist), recently approved for menopausal hot flushes, targets the KNDy neuron system — these neurons also thermoregulate, explaining why their dysfunction at menopause drives vasomotor symptoms.

what is fezolinetant and its use for men: Fezolinetant (Veoza, brand name in EU) is a neurokinin 3 (NK3) receptor antagonist approved in 2023 (FDA and EMA) for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes and night sweats) in menopause. It is the first non-hormonal, non-SSRI/SNRI targeted therapy for menopausal hot flushes. Mechanism: at menopause, declining estrogen removes inhibition of KNDy (kisspeptin-neurokinin B-dynorphin) neurons in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus. These neurons, when hyperactivated, project to thermoregulatory centres, causing inappropriate heat dissipation (hot flushes and night sweats). Fezolinetant blocks NK3 receptors on these neurons, suppressing the signal. In SKYLIGHT 1 and 2 phase 3 trials, fezolinetant significantly reduced hot flush frequency and severity compared to placebo, with effects seen within 1 week. It is an important advance for women who cannot or prefer not to use HRT.

what is the role of the cervical microbi: The healthy cervical and vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species (particularly L. crispatus, L. iners, L. gasseri, L. jensenii), which produce lactic acid and hydrogen peroxide, maintaining pH 3.8 to 4.5. A Lactobacillus-dominant microbiome is associated with better IVF implantation rates, lower risk of preterm birth, and protection against STIs. Dysbiosis — replacement of Lactobacillus with a diverse anaerobic community (as in bacterial vaginosis) — is associated with impaired IVF outcomes, higher miscarriage risk, preterm birth (BV during pregnancy triples preterm birth risk), higher STI acquisition risk, and worsened endometriosis. Uterine microbiome research (the EMMA and ALICE tests — endometrial microbiome metagenomic analysis and analysis of infectious chronic endometritis) aims to identify whether uterine microbiome abnormalities (including non-Lactobacillus-dominant uterine microbiome or chronic endometritis) contribute to implantation failure — the evidence for clinical utility of these tests is emerging but not yet definitive.

what is chronic endometritis and its eff: Chronic endometritis (CE) is a persistent, low-grade infection of the endometrial lining by bacteria (most commonly Enterococcus, Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, Enterobacter, Klebsiella, and Ureaplasma urealyticum). Unlike acute endometritis (which causes severe symptoms), CE is largely asymptomatic or causes only subtle symptoms (mild pelvic pain, irregular bleeding, unusual discharge). It is found in approximately 14% of infertile women and up to 30% of women with recurrent miscarriage or implantation failure. CE is diagnosed by endometrial biopsy and CD138 immunohistochemistry (identifying plasma cells in the endometrium, which are not normally present). Treatment is antibiotic therapy (doxycycline 14 days, or extended triple antibiotic regimens for polymicrobial infection), which typically eradicates CE and significantly improves subsequent IVF implantation and live birth rates in affected women.

what is the endometrial receptivity arra: The endometrial receptivity array (ERA) is a genomic test that analyses the expression of 248 genes in an endometrial biopsy to determine the 'receptive' status of the endometrium — whether it is in its optimal state for embryo implantation. The window of implantation (WOI) occurs approximately 5 to 10 days after ovulation and is timed relative to progesterone exposure in FET (frozen embryo transfer) cycles. Most women have a WOI at the standard timing, but approximately 25 to 30% of women with recurrent implantation failure are 'displaced' — their WOI is earlier or later than expected. The ERA test identifies the personalised WOI and allows transfer timing to be adjusted. A 2021 randomised controlled trial (IGENOMIX ERA trial) showed no benefit of ERA-guided transfer in women having their first IVF cycle with a predicted normal WOI. Its utility appears to be specifically in recurrent implantation failure, where personalising transfer timing has improved outcomes in observational studies. Routine use in all IVF patients is not supported by current evidence.

what is a poor ovarian responder in IVF: A poor ovarian responder (POR) is someone who produces fewer follicles than expected in response to standard gonadotropin stimulation for IVF. The Bologna criteria (ESHRE 2011) define POR as meeting at least two of three criteria: advanced maternal age (≥40) or a risk factor for POR; a previous poor ovarian response (≤3 oocytes with a standard stimulation protocol); or an abnormal ovarian reserve test (AMH <1.1 pmol/L, AFC <5 to 7). Poor response is associated with fewer eggs retrieved, fewer embryos available for transfer, and lower cumulative live birth rates. Management strategies vary: POSEIDON classification (Patients Outcomes Simulators for Egg Donation in Infertile Patients) sub-classifies POR to guide individualised treatment. Approaches include maximal gonadotropin doses (though limited evidence that very high doses improve outcomes), androgen priming (DHEA or testosterone patch before stimulation), growth hormone co-treatment (modest evidence for improved outcomes in POR), luteal phase stimulation protocols, and progressing directly to donor egg IVF when appropriate.

what is gonorrhoea resistant to antibiot: Antimicrobial-resistant gonorrhoea is a serious and growing public health concern. Neisseria gonorrhoeae has developed resistance to multiple antibiotic classes: penicillin, tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin), and most recently azithromycin (previously part of dual therapy). Extensive drug-resistant (XDR) gonorrhoea — resistant to ceftriaxone (the current last-line agent) — has been reported in multiple countries. The UK currently uses ceftriaxone 1g IM monotherapy as first-line treatment, with test of cure at 2 weeks mandatory for all cases. Dual antibiotic therapy (ceftriaxone plus azithromycin) was abandoned in 2019 in the UK because azithromycin resistance was driving concern. New antibiotics and treatment combinations are in development. The response requires rigorous contact tracing, resistance surveillance, mandatory test of cure, and reduced antibiotic prescription pressure across all sectors.

what is pre-conception health and folic: Pre-conception health optimisation significantly improves pregnancy outcomes. The most evidence-based intervention is folic acid supplementation: 400 micrograms (0.4 mg) daily starting at least 1 month before conception and continuing through the first 12 weeks of pregnancy reduces the risk of neural tube defects (spina bifida, anencephaly) by approximately 70%. Women at higher risk (personal or family history of NTD, epilepsy medications that affect folate metabolism, diabetes, BMI >30) are recommended 5 mg folic acid daily. Additional pre-conception recommendations: ensure rubella immunity (vaccination if not immune, then avoid conception for 1 month after MMR); check thyroid function (treat subclinical hypothyroidism to TSH <2.5 mIU/L); optimise weight (BMI between 18.5 and 30 improves outcomes); stop smoking and limit alcohol; review all medications for pregnancy safety; take vitamin D (10 mcg daily); ensure cervical screening is up to date; and treat any identified infections (STIs, BV) before conception.

what is hyperemesis gravidarum: Hyperemesis gravidarum (HG) is severe, persistent nausea and vomiting in pregnancy — distinct from the common morning sickness that affects approximately 70 to 80% of pregnant people. HG is defined by significant weight loss (typically >5% of pre-pregnancy body weight), dehydration, ketonuria (ketones in urine from starvation), and often requires hospitalisation. It affects approximately 1 to 3% of pregnancies and is one of the leading causes of hospitalisation in the first trimester. The cause is not fully understood but is strongly linked to high or rapidly rising hCG levels (multiple pregnancies and molar pregnancies, which produce very high hCG, are associated with more severe symptoms), and possibly sensitivity to hCG in the nausea/vomiting centre (area postrema). It is associated with thyroid hormone dysregulation (gestational transient thyrotoxicosis — hCG has TSH-like activity). Treatment: antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine), IV fluids and electrolyte replacement, thiamine supplementation (Wernicke's encephalopathy is a serious complication of prolonged vomiting), and in severe cases nutritional support. HG has significant mental health impacts and is associated with PTSD and depression.

what is symphysis pubis dysfunction in p: Symphysis pubis dysfunction (SPD), now classified as a subtype of pelvic girdle pain (PGP), involves pain and instability at the symphysis pubis (the joint at the front of the pelvis where the two pubic bones meet). It is caused by increased joint laxity from relaxin and progesterone in pregnancy, combined with the mechanical load of the growing uterus. Symptoms include sharp pubic pain that radiates to the inner thighs and groin, a clicking or grinding sensation at the pubic joint, difficulty walking (a waddling gait), pain with any leg-separation movement (climbing stairs, getting in and out of a car, turning in bed, sexual intercourse). Severe cases can be temporarily disabling. Management includes pelvic support belts (reduces pelvic gap), physiotherapy (strengthening pelvic girdle stability without further stressing the symphysis), avoiding asymmetric loading (keeping knees together when moving), walking aids in severe cases, and analgesics. It typically resolves within weeks of delivery, though a minority have persistent symptoms.

what is placenta accreta spectrum: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) describes abnormal placental attachment where the placenta invades into or through the uterine wall beyond the normal decidual layer. Three types by depth: accreta (invades the myometrium — most common), increta (through the myometrium), and percreta (through the uterine serosa and potentially into adjacent organs such as the bladder). PAS is most strongly associated with a previous caesarean scar combined with a low-lying or previa placenta — the rate increases dramatically with multiple prior caesarean sections (from <1% with no prior CS to approximately 67% with placenta praevia and 4 prior caesareans). PAS is associated with catastrophic haemorrhage at delivery. Management requires antenatal diagnosis by ultrasound and MRI, delivery in a specialist centre with a multidisciplinary team, and typically planned hysterectomy at delivery. Conservative management (leaving the placenta in situ and waiting for resorption) is occasionally considered for those who strongly desire future fertility, but carries risk of haemorrhage and infection.

what is HELLP syndrome: HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe pregnancy complication closely related to pre-eclampsia but can also occur without it. It typically presents between 28 and 36 weeks gestation or postpartum. Symptoms include: severe right upper quadrant or epigastric pain (from hepatic swelling), nausea and vomiting, headache, malaise, and visual disturbances. Blood tests show haemolysis (elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, anaemia), elevated ALT and AST (liver enzymes), and thrombocytopenia (platelet count below 100 × 10⁹/L). HELLP can rapidly deteriorate to hepatic rupture, DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation), abruptio placentae, acute renal failure, and maternal death. Treatment requires hospitalisation, stabilisation, magnesium sulphate (seizure prophylaxis), corticosteroids (for fetal lung maturity), antihypertensives, and urgent delivery — HELLP does not resolve until the placenta is delivered.

what are the signs of pre-eclampsia: Pre-eclampsia is a pregnancy complication characterised by hypertension (blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg on two occasions 4 hours apart) after 20 weeks gestation, combined with proteinuria (protein in urine) and/or organ dysfunction. Warning signs: new headache not relieved by paracetamol, visual disturbances (flashing lights, blurred vision), epigastric pain, rapidly worsening oedema (face, hands, ankles), sudden significant weight gain from fluid retention, and reduced or absent fetal movements. Risk factors include first pregnancy, pre-existing hypertension, diabetes, renal disease, obesity, multiple pregnancy, prior pre-eclampsia, and antiphospholipid syndrome. In high-risk women, low-dose aspirin (150 mg nightly from 12 to 36 weeks) significantly reduces the risk of pre-eclampsia — NICE recommends aspirin prophylaxis based on the Fetal Medicine Foundation risk algorithm. Pre-eclampsia ranges from mild (managed with monitoring and antihypertensives) to severe/eclamptic (requiring urgent delivery).

what is polycystic ovary morphology and: Polycystic ovary morphology (PCOM) refers to the ultrasound appearance of the ovaries — a large ovarian volume (>10 ml) and/or increased antral follicle count (≥20 follicles per ovary by the ESHRE/ASRM 2023 updated Rotterdam criteria, revised from the prior threshold of ≥12) — without necessarily fulfilling criteria for PCOS. PCOM alone (without irregular periods or elevated androgens) is not diagnosed as PCOS — it is a descriptive finding present in approximately 20 to 33% of reproductive-age women, many of whom have completely normal cycles, fertility, and hormone levels. PCOS requires at least 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligo/anovulation (irregular periods), clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and PCOM. PCOM in isolation is not pathological but may represent a spectrum toward PCOS in those with additional risk factors or subclinical hormonal changes.

what is dienogest for endometriosis and: Dienogest is a progestogen (selective progesterone receptor agonist) specifically developed for endometriosis and adenomyosis treatment. It is available in Europe and widely used globally, though not licensed in all countries. Mechanism: it suppresses endometrial growth by opposing estrogen at the endometrial level, creating a hypoestrogenic, locally anti-inflammatory environment. Unlike GnRH agonists, dienogest does not cause complete estrogen suppression — it produces a 'pseudo-decidualisation' of endometriotic tissue, causing atrophy with manageable estrogen levels (avoiding severe menopausal symptoms and bone density loss). Clinical evidence: Dienogest (2 mg daily) is as effective as GnRH agonist therapy for endometriosis pain with a better tolerability and bone density profile. For adenomyosis, it significantly reduces volume, dysmenorrhea, and heavy bleeding. Common side effects include irregular spotting (particularly in the first 3 to 6 months), low mood, acne, and bloating. It is an alternative to the combined pill, progestogen-only pill, or GnRH agonists for long-term medical endometriosis management.

what is the difference between endometri: Two surgical approaches exist for treating endometriosis during laparoscopy: excision (cutting out lesions) and ablation (destroying lesions by laser, diathermy, or plasma energy without removing the underlying tissue). Excision removes the full depth of the lesion and provides tissue for histological confirmation. Ablation destroys the surface but may leave deeper disease, and provides no tissue for diagnosis. Evidence strongly favours excision over ablation: a landmark RCT (Abbott et al., 2004) showed excision of visible endometriosis significantly reduced pain scores compared to diagnostic laparoscopy alone, with effect size maintained at 5-year follow-up. Ablation trials have shown less consistent benefit. BSGE (British Society for Gynaecological Endoscopy) and ESHRE guidelines recommend excision as the preferred surgical technique. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (involving the uterosacral ligaments, bowel, bladder, or ureters) requires specialist multidisciplinary surgical excision at a BSGE-accredited endometriosis centre.

what is low-dose naltrexone for endometr: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN — typically 1.5 to 4.5 mg at night, far below the standard 50 mg dose used for opioid dependence) is an emerging experimental treatment being investigated for endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain. At low doses, naltrexone transiently blocks opioid receptors, triggering a rebound overproduction of endogenous opioids (endorphins) and upregulating opioid receptor sensitivity — producing analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. It also modulates microglia (central nervous system immune cells) and reduces neuroinflammation, theoretically relevant to the central sensitisation component of endometriosis pain. Evidence: small observational studies and a pilot RCT suggest LDN reduces chronic pain scores in fibromyalgia and Crohn's disease. Specific endometriosis RCTs are underway. LDN has a favourable side effect profile (vivid dreams most commonly reported, particularly at initiation). It is not currently licensed for this use but is prescribed off-label in specialist centres.

what are pelvic floor exercises and how: Pelvic floor exercises (Kegel exercises) strengthen the levator ani muscle group — the muscles that form a hammock from the pubic bone to the tailbone, supporting the bladder, uterus, and bowel. To identify the pelvic floor: try to stop the flow of urine midstream (do this only for identification, not as a regular exercise — stopping midstream habitually can impair bladder function). Once identified, exercise technique: sit, stand, or lie comfortably. Squeeze and lift the pelvic floor muscles upward and inward (not the buttocks, thighs, or abdomen). Hold for 3 to 10 seconds, then fully relax for an equal period — complete relaxation is as important as the contraction. Perform 8 to 12 repetitions three times daily. Progressive increase in hold time (up to 10 seconds) and adding quick flick contractions (rapidly squeezing and releasing) improves both slow-twitch (endurance) and fast-twitch (quick response to cough/sneeze) muscle fibres. Results require 3 to 6 months of consistent practice. A pelvic floor physiotherapist can assess technique, as many people unintentionally bear down (pushing rather than lifting).

what is the difference between a hyperto: A hypertonic (overactive or tight) pelvic floor is one in which muscles are chronically contracted, tense, or unable to fully relax. Symptoms include pelvic pain, deep dyspareunia, vaginismus, difficulty inserting tampons, vulvodynia, difficulty initiating urination or bowel movements, and paradoxical urinary incontinence (the tight muscles tire and leak). Treatment focuses on relaxation: diaphragmatic breathing, progressive relaxation techniques, manual trigger point release by a pelvic floor physiotherapist, vaginal dilators, and addressing psychological contributors (anxiety, hypervigilance). A hypotonic (weak) pelvic floor has insufficient muscle tone, causing stress urinary incontinence, pelvic organ prolapse, reduced orgasmic sensation, and difficulty sustaining bladder and bowel control. Treatment focuses on strengthening: progressive pelvic floor exercises, biofeedback, and neuromuscular electrical stimulation. Many people have mixed presentations (some tight muscles and some weak ones in different areas) — this is why pelvic floor physiotherapy assessment is far more useful than generic Kegel exercise advice.

what is diastasis recti recovery after b: Diastasis recti (DR) is the separation of the two rectus abdominis muscles along the linea alba, present in virtually all pregnancies by the third trimester. Most mild-moderate cases narrow substantially by 6 to 8 weeks postpartum. Clinical assessment uses a finger-width measure at rest and under load (e.g., a gentle head lift). A persistent gap >2 cm at the umbilicus, combined with poor tissue tension (a 'squishy' rather than taut linea alba), is clinically significant. Rehabilitation principles: early postpartum (0 to 6 weeks) focuses on gentle diaphragmatic breathing, early pelvic floor activation, and avoiding activities that cause the abdomen to dome or cone. From 6 to 12 weeks, progressive core loading (dead bugs, heel slides, side-lying exercises). Return to traditional sit-ups, crunches, full planks, and high-impact activities requires assessment of inter-recti distance and linea alba tension — not a fixed time point. Traditional sit-ups increase intra-abdominal pressure and inter-recti gap and are contraindicated until linea alba tension is restored. Surgical repair (abdominoplasty with fascial plication) is considered for severe, persistent DR causing functional impairment.

what is vasa praevia and why is it dange: Vasa praevia is a rare (1 in 2,500 pregnancies) but potentially fatal fetal condition in which unprotected fetal blood vessels (not enclosed in the umbilical cord or placenta) run across the internal cervical os. When the membranes rupture (naturally or artificially), these vessels can tear, causing rapid fetal exsanguination (bleeding of fetal blood) — the fetal blood volume is only approximately 200 to 300 ml, so even modest blood loss can be catastrophic. Fetal mortality without prior diagnosis approaches 50 to 75%; with antenatal diagnosis and planned elective caesarean before membrane rupture, survival is over 97%. Risk factors: velamentous cord insertion (umbilical cord inserts into the membranes rather than the placenta), bilobed or succenturiate lobe placenta, IVF pregnancies, and low-lying placenta. Antenatal diagnosis is by transvaginal colour Doppler ultrasound — it is recommended that all women with a low-lying placenta or IVF conception have targeted vasa praevia screening at the 20-week anomaly scan.

what is the intrauterine growth restrict: Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), also called fetal growth restriction (FGR), is when a fetus fails to reach its genetically determined growth potential. It is diagnosed when estimated fetal weight (EFW) or abdominal circumference is below the 10th centile for gestational age, with associated Doppler evidence of placental insufficiency (reduced or absent end-diastolic flow in the umbilical artery). FGR carries serious risks: intrauterine fetal death, preterm birth, neonatal complications (hypoglycaemia, polycythaemia, hypothermia), and long-term health effects (increased cardiovascular disease and metabolic disease risk in adulthood — the Barker hypothesis of fetal programming). Causes include placental insufficiency (most common — from pre-eclampsia, antiphospholipid syndrome, thrombophilias), fetal chromosomal abnormalities (particularly trisomy 13, 18), infections (CMV, toxoplasma), maternal severe undernutrition, smoking, and substance use. Management involves intensified surveillance (serial growth scans, umbilical artery Doppler) and timely delivery before fetal compromise — timing balances prematurity risks against the risk of intrauterine death.

what is recurrent vulvovaginal candidias: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) is defined as four or more confirmed Candida infections per year. It affects approximately 5 to 8% of women of reproductive age. The vast majority of cases (85 to 90%) are caused by Candida albicans, with a minority by non-albicans species (C. glabrata, C. tropicalis, C. parapsilosis) — the latter are more resistant to standard azole antifungal treatment. Contributing factors include: uncontrolled diabetes mellitus (elevated glucose provides a substrate for Candida growth); immunosuppression (HIV, corticosteroids, chemotherapy); antibiotic use (disrupts Lactobacillus dominance, allowing Candida overgrowth); high-dose estrogen contraception (estrogen promotes Candida adhesion); tight synthetic clothing (warmth and moisture); and genetic susceptibility (variants in antifungal immune genes including STAT3 and IL-17 signalling). RVVC is not sexually transmitted and is not an indication for partner treatment. Management: induction therapy (oral fluconazole 150 mg three doses, one week apart), followed by 6-month suppressive maintenance (weekly oral fluconazole 150 mg) — reduces recurrence in approximately 90% during maintenance, but 50% relapse when suppression is withdrawn. Boric acid vaginal pessaries are second-line for azole-resistant non-albicans species.

does eczema worsen with the menstrual cy: Yes — approximately 30 to 40% of women with atopic eczema (atopic dermatitis) report cyclical worsening of their skin condition in the premenstrual period, a phenomenon called catamenial or premenstrual eczema. The mechanism involves the immunomodulatory properties of estrogen and progesterone: the premenstrual drop in both hormones triggers a pro-inflammatory Th1 shift in the immune system (the same shift that mediates menstruation) which amplifies atopic inflammation. Progesterone withdrawal specifically has been shown to worsen skin barrier function and increase transepidermal water loss. Some women with severe premenstrual eczema flares may have progesterone allergy (autoimmune progesterone dermatitis) — a rare but distinct condition in which intradermal testing with progesterone produces a reaction. For cyclical eczema, maintaining consistent skin barrier management (frequent emollient use, short warm showers, avoiding triggers) is important across the whole cycle, with intensified treatment during the premenstrual window.

does psoriasis worsen with the menstrual: Psoriasis — a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin condition — shows cyclical variation in approximately 40 to 55% of affected women. Flares most commonly occur in the premenstrual or menstrual phase. The mechanism involves the pro-inflammatory premenstrual hormonal shift: as estrogen declines, the anti-inflammatory Th2 bias maintained by estrogen is lost, and the characteristic Th17/Th1 inflammatory profile of psoriasis is amplified. Conversely, during pregnancy (sustained high estrogen and progesterone), psoriasis often dramatically improves in 40 to 60% of women, only to flare postpartum when hormones fall rapidly. The same pattern of improvement during the follicular phase (rising estrogen) and worsening in the late luteal and menstrual phase is reported by many women. Treatment of cyclical psoriasis flares: consistent use of topical treatments, and when systemic treatment is used, ensuring it is compatible with any planned pregnancy (methotrexate, acitretin, and some biologics require washout periods before conception).

does rosacea worsen with the menstrual c: Rosacea — a chronic inflammatory skin condition causing facial redness, flushing, telangiectasia, and sometimes acne-like pustules — frequently fluctuates with the menstrual cycle. Hormonal changes, particularly the perimenstrual drop in estrogen and the vasomotor instability this causes, trigger or worsen the facial flushing characteristic of rosacea. Progesterone, which has vasodilatory effects, also peaks in the luteal phase before withdrawal, potentially amplifying flushing episodes. Rosacea is significantly more common in perimenopausal and menopausal women — the estrogen withdrawal of menopause overlaps with rosacea pathophysiology (vasomotor instability, altered skin barrier function, altered sebaceous gland activity). Women with rosacea often track their worst flares around menstruation and ovulation. Managing rosacea triggers is central to treatment alongside topical azelaic acid, metronidazole, or brimonidine; systemic doxycycline for inflammatory rosacea; and vascular laser therapy for telangiectasia.

what is premenstrual asthma: Premenstrual asthma is cyclical worsening of asthma in the premenstrual and early menstrual phase. It affects approximately 20 to 40% of women with asthma. Mechanisms: estrogen and progesterone both have bronchodilatory properties — their withdrawal premenstrually allows increased bronchospasm. Progesterone stimulates respiratory drive and increases sensitivity to hypercapnia; its withdrawal causes reduced respiratory muscle efficiency. Cyclical prostaglandin release during menstruation, particularly PGF2-alpha (a potent bronchoconstrictor), directly triggers bronchospasm. Heightened general premenstrual inflammation may amplify airway inflammation. Diagnosis: peak flow monitoring across the cycle identifies the cyclical pattern. Management: ensuring baseline asthma is optimally controlled with regular inhaled corticosteroids; short-acting bronchodilator as needed during premenstrual phase; in severe cases, hormonal approaches to eliminate the cyclical hormonal drop (continuous OCP, GnRH agonist) have shown benefit in case series.

what is menstrual migraine and how is it: Menstrual migraine is migraine occurring exclusively or predominantly around menstruation, typically from 2 days before to 3 days after the start of the period (the perimenstrual window). Pure menstrual migraine occurs only in this window; menstrual-related migraine also occurs at other cycle times. The trigger is the premenstrual drop in estrogen, which causes cerebrovascular changes and activates trigeminal pain pathways. Menstrual migraines are typically longer, more severe, and less responsive to standard triptans than non-menstrual migraines, and are more likely to cause disability. Treatment options: acute treatment with triptans (frovatriptan, naratriptan, or zolmitriptan are preferred for menstrual migraine because of their longer half-lives, reducing headache recurrence); short-cycle perimenstrual prophylaxis (frovatriptan or naratriptan taken twice daily for 5 to 6 days around menstruation, starting 2 days before expected onset) — this significantly reduces menstrual migraine frequency. Hormonal approaches for prevention include continuous combined OCP (avoiding the pill-free week drop in estrogen), transdermal estrogen (Estradot patch or estrogel) applied perimenstrually in non-contraception users.

what is epilepsy and the menstrual cycle: Catamenial epilepsy is increased seizure frequency or severity in a pattern correlated with the menstrual cycle. Three catamenial patterns are recognised: perimenstrual (seizures cluster around menstruation, when estrogen and progesterone fall), periovulatory (seizures cluster around ovulation, when estrogen peaks), and luteal (seizures increase across the entire luteal phase in women with inadequate luteal phase progesterone rise). Estrogen is generally pro-convulsant (lowers seizure threshold) and progesterone is generally anti-convulsant (its neurosteroid metabolite allopregnanolone enhances GABA-A receptor activity, inhibiting neuronal excitability). The cyclical fall in progesterone premenstrually reduces GABAergic inhibition, lowering seizure threshold. Treatment options for catamenial epilepsy: optimising baseline anticonvulsant therapy; clobazam (a benzodiazepine) taken perimenstrually as add-on therapy; progesterone supplementation (cyclic natural progesterone) in the luteal phase; acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) perimenstrually. Hormonal contraception may improve or worsen catamenial epilepsy depending on the individual and the progestogen used.

what are the effects of antidepressants: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors — fluoxetine, sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine) can affect the menstrual cycle in several ways. They may alter cycle length, though evidence is inconsistent. A more consistent finding is that SSRIs reduce sexual desire, arousal, and orgasm (a side effect affecting approximately 40 to 70% of users) — this occurs through serotonergic inhibition of dopaminergic pathways involved in sexual motivation. SSRIs reduce PMDD symptoms profoundly — both continuous daily dosing and symptom-onset dosing (starting 2 weeks before the period, stopping at the onset of menstruation) are effective for PMDD. Paroxetine can cause galactorrhoea and irregular periods through mild dopamine antagonism (raising prolactin). When used in pregnancy, SSRIs (particularly paroxetine) are associated with small risks to the fetus and neonatal adaptation syndrome, but untreated severe depression or anxiety in pregnancy also carries risks — the decision involves weighing benefits and risks.

what is antipsychotic medication and its: Antipsychotic medications — particularly typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) and some atypical antipsychotics (risperidone, amisulpride) — block dopamine D2 receptors in the pituitary gland, causing hyperprolactinaemia (elevated prolactin). Prolactin suppresses GnRH pulsatility, leading to: oligomenorrhoea (infrequent periods) or amenorrhoea, anovulation (absent ovulation), reduced fertility, galactorrhoea (milk production outside pregnancy), vaginal dryness, and reduced libido. Hyperprolactinaemia also reduces estrogen chronically, increasing osteoporosis risk. Management: switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic (quetiapine, clozapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole — which are less prone to prolactin elevation); aripiprazole can be added to a prolactin-raising antipsychotic to offset its D2-blocking effects (aripiprazole is a partial D2 agonist that normalises prolactin); dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine) can be used to reduce prolactin but may worsen psychosis and require specialist oversight.

what is the effect of anticoagulants on: Anticoagulants (warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants — apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban — and low molecular weight heparin) significantly increase menstrual blood loss. Heavy menstrual bleeding is the most common clinically significant bleeding complication in women taking anticoagulants, affecting approximately 60 to 70% of women. This occurs because coagulation is required for normal haemostasis within the endometrium at menstruation — anticoagulants impair this local clotting, dramatically increasing blood loss volume and duration. Management options for women requiring anticoagulation: the levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena) is the most effective intervention, reducing blood loss by 70 to 90% through local progestogenic endometrial thinning — it does not interact with anticoagulants; the norethisterone oral pill taken continuously; tranexamic acid during menstruation (reduces blood loss by 30 to 50% via anti-fibrinolytic mechanism); or NSAIDs (reduce prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation). Any anticoagulated woman with heavy periods should be under joint care of a gynaecologist and haematologist or anticoagulation specialist.

what is the effect of chemotherapy on th: Chemotherapy for cancer treatment profoundly affects the menstrual cycle and fertility. Chemotherapy drugs (particularly alkylating agents — cyclophosphamide, busulfan, melphalan; and platinum compounds — cisplatin, carboplatin) are most damaging to ovarian primordial follicles, causing accelerated follicle loss. This causes: premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) — complete cessation of ovarian function — in a proportion of women, with the risk depending on drug class, dose, and patient age (younger women have more follicle reserve and lower risk). Temporary amenorrhoea during treatment is common. After chemotherapy, periods may return (indicating some preserved ovarian function) or may not. Pre-treatment fertility preservation (egg freezing or embryo freezing before chemotherapy) is the most evidence-based strategy to preserve future fertility and should be discussed with all pre-menopausal women before starting potentially gonadotoxic chemotherapy. GnRH agonist co-treatment during chemotherapy (ovarian suppression) may offer some ovarioprotection in premenopausal women with breast cancer and is recommended by ASCO guidelines for this indication.

what is premature ovarian insufficiency: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) — previously called premature ovarian failure — is the cessation of normal ovarian function before age 40. It is characterised by: amenorrhoea, elevated FSH (>25 IU/L on two occasions 4 weeks apart), low estrogen, and often low AMH. POI affects approximately 1 in 100 women before 40, 1 in 1000 before 30, and 1 in 10,000 before 20. The term 'failure' is avoided because in spontaneous POI, ovarian function can fluctuate — up to 5 to 10% of women with POI conceive spontaneously, unlike in natural menopause which is irreversible. Causes: 30% are autoimmune (associated with other autoimmune conditions — thyroid disease, Addison's disease, type 1 diabetes); chromosomal (Turner syndrome, fragile X premutation carriers); iatrogenic (surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy); and idiopathic (cause unknown) in the majority. Management: HRT until the average age of natural menopause (51) is strongly recommended — POI without HRT dramatically increases cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, cognitive decline, and all-cause mortality risk. POI is profoundly different from natural menopause — the health consequences are more severe and the need for HRT more urgent.

what is Turner syndrome and its effects: Turner syndrome (TS) is a chromosomal condition affecting females in which one X chromosome is fully or partially absent (45,X is the most common karyotype; mosaicism — 45,X/46,XX or 45,X/46,XY — accounts for many of the remainder). It affects approximately 1 in 2,000 female births. Characteristic features: short stature, webbed neck, lymphoedema, cardiac malformations (bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation of the aorta), wide-set nipples, and gonadal dysgenesis. Reproductive impact: most women with Turner syndrome have streak gonads (fibrous remnants rather than functional ovaries) and develop POI — typically before or during puberty, presenting as delayed puberty or primary amenorrhoea. Approximately 70 to 80% of TS women will not have spontaneous puberty and require hormone replacement to induce puberty and prevent osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. Spontaneous fertility is rare but occurs in approximately 2% (mostly in mosaic karyotypes). Options for pregnancy include IVF with donor eggs (pregnancy in TS carries increased cardiac risk — pre-pregnancy cardiac evaluation is essential) or fertility preservation of spontaneously developing follicles where possible.

what is fragile X premutation and ovaria: The FMR1 gene (fragile X mental retardation 1) on the X chromosome normally has 5 to 44 CGG trinucleotide repeats. In fragile X syndrome, there are >200 repeats. The premutation range (55 to 200 repeats) is a distinct condition — women with FMR1 premutations have a 20% lifetime risk of premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), compared to 1% in the general population. This is the most common known genetic cause of POI. The mechanism: abnormal FMR1 premutation mRNA is toxic to granulosa cells. Premutation carriers may also develop fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) in later life. Because premutation carriers can pass the gene to children (who may have the full mutation causing fragile X syndrome), genetic counselling before conception is important. All women with unexplained POI should be offered FMR1 premutation testing.

what is congenital adrenal hyperplasia a: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is a group of autosomal recessive conditions caused by enzyme deficiencies in cortisol synthesis, resulting in ACTH overdriving the adrenal glands and excess androgens being produced. The most common form is 21-hydroxylase deficiency (CYP21A2 mutations), accounting for 90 to 95% of cases. Classic CAH (severe): presents at birth with genital ambiguity in XX individuals (virilisation from excess prenatal androgens) and potentially life-threatening salt-wasting adrenal crisis. Non-classic CAH (NCAH): a milder, late-onset form presenting in adolescence or adulthood. In women with NCAH, clinical features include: oligomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea (androgen excess suppresses GnRH pulsatility); hirsutism and acne; reduced fertility due to anovulation; and sometimes rapid clitoromegaly. NCAH clinically resembles PCOS and must be distinguished by measuring 17-hydroxyprogesterone (elevated at baseline or after ACTH stimulation in CAH). Treatment: glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone or prednisolone) replacement suppresses ACTH excess, reduces excess androgens, and can restore menstrual regularity and fertility.

what is the impact of obesity on menstru: Obesity (BMI >30) significantly disrupts menstrual and reproductive health through multiple mechanisms. Adipose tissue is an endocrine organ that aromatises androgens to estrogens — excess adipose tissue produces excess estrogen, which chronically suppresses FSH and LH pulsatility, disrupting follicle development and causing oligo/anovulation. Obesity also drives insulin resistance and hyperinsulinaemia, which increases ovarian androgen production (similar to PCOS — in fact, obesity is one of the most important environmental drivers of PCOS phenotype). Consequences for the menstrual cycle: irregular or absent periods, heavy menstrual bleeding (excess estrogen chronically stimulates the endometrium without the counterbalancing progesterone of ovulation), and elevated risk of endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer. Fertility: anovulation is the most direct mechanism; obesity also impairs embryo quality and reduces IVF success rates independently of anovulation. Weight loss of 5 to 10% in women with overweight and PCOS restores ovulation in approximately 50 to 80% without medication. Bariatric surgery dramatically improves menstrual regularity and fertility.

what is endometrial hyperplasia and when: Endometrial hyperplasia is abnormal thickening of the uterine lining due to excessive estrogen stimulation without adequate progestogen opposition. Causes: obesity, PCOS (chronic anovulation means no progesterone-producing corpus luteum), unopposed estrogen therapy, tamoxifen, early perimenopause with erratic ovulation, and estrogen-secreting tumours. The classification system (WHO 2014) distinguishes: hyperplasia without atypia (no pre-cancerous cell changes — low risk of progression to cancer, approximately 2 to 3%; most regress with progestogen treatment) and atypical hyperplasia/endometrioid intraepithelial neoplasia (EIN) (significant cytological atypia — up to 25 to 43% risk of co-existing or subsequent endometrial cancer; hysterectomy is the recommended treatment in women who have completed their family; high-dose progestogen with close surveillance in those desiring future fertility). Symptoms: abnormal uterine bleeding (heavy periods, irregular bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding). Diagnosis by endometrial biopsy (Pipelle aspirate) or hysteroscopy with directed biopsy.

what is endometrial cancer and what are: Endometrial cancer (uterine cancer) is the most common gynaecological cancer in high-income countries, with approximately 10,000 new diagnoses per year in the UK. Risk factors include: oestrogen excess (obesity, PCOS, nulliparity, late menopause, tamoxifen use, hormone-producing ovarian tumours); endometrial hyperplasia with atypia; Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer — HNPCC); diabetes and hypertension; and personal history of breast or colon cancer. Type 1 endometrial cancers (endometrioid, estrogen-driven, lower grade — accounting for 80% of cases) are associated with these risk factors and have relatively good prognosis. Type 2 endometrial cancers (serous, clear cell — not estrogen-driven, higher grade, worse prognosis) account for 20%. The cardinal symptom is postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) — all PMB must be urgently investigated. In pre-menopausal women: intermenstrual bleeding, irregular heavy bleeding, or worsening menorrhagia, especially in women with obesity or PCOS, warrants investigation. Treatment: hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) is curative for early-stage disease.

what is cervical cancer and how is it st: Cervical cancer is caused by persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV), most commonly HPV 16 and 18. It affects approximately 3,200 women per year in the UK. The most common histological type is squamous cell carcinoma (approximately 70 to 75%), followed by adenocarcinoma (approximately 20 to 25%). Symptoms: in early disease, often none (detected by screening); later — abnormal bleeding (post-coital bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding), abnormal discharge, pelvic pain. FIGO staging (2018): Stage I — confined to the cervix; Stage II — beyond the cervix but not to pelvic wall or lower third of vagina; Stage III — extends to pelvic wall, lower vagina, or causes hydronephrosis; Stage IV — involves bladder, bowel, or distant metastases. Treatment depends on stage: Stage IA1 — cone biopsy or simple hysterectomy; Stage IA2 to IB2 — radical hysterectomy (Wertheim's) with pelvic lymphadenectomy, or chemoradiotherapy; Stage IB3 and above — primary chemoradiotherapy with cisplatin is preferred. Fertility-sparing surgery (radical trachelectomy — removal of the cervix and upper vagina while preserving the uterus) is possible in Stage IA2 to IB1 disease for women who wish to preserve fertility.

what is vulvar cancer: Vulvar cancer affects the external female genitalia — labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, and perineum. It accounts for approximately 4 to 5% of gynaecological cancers in the UK. There are two distinct pathways: type 1 is HPV-related (squamous cell carcinoma preceded by high-grade vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia — VIN), affecting younger women; type 2 is non-HPV-related (differentiated VIN, associated with lichen sclerosus), typically affecting older women. Symptoms: persistent vulvar itch, soreness, pain, lump, ulcer, skin change (thickening, whitening, or redness), or a wart-like growth. Any persistent unexplained vulvar lesion should be biopsied. Vulvar lichen sclerosus, a common, treatable chronic inflammatory skin condition of the vulva, is associated with an approximately 4 to 5% lifetime risk of vulvar squamous cell carcinoma — it should be diagnosed, treated adequately with potent topical corticosteroids, and monitored regularly. Treatment of vulvar cancer: surgical excision with clear margins (wide local excision or radical vulvectomy), lymph node assessment, and radiotherapy depending on stage.

what is vaginal cancer: Primary vaginal cancer (cancer arising in the vaginal wall rather than spreading from the cervix or vulva) is rare, accounting for approximately 1 to 2% of gynaecological cancers. The majority are squamous cell carcinoma (related to HPV infection, most commonly in the upper third of the vagina). Rarer types include clear cell adenocarcinoma (historically linked to in utero DES — diethylstilboestrol — exposure in the 1950s to 70s in the US) and vaginal melanoma. Symptoms: abnormal vaginal bleeding (postmenopausal, post-coital, or intermenstrual), vaginal discharge, pelvic or vaginal pain, and a palpable mass. Diagnosis: speculum examination, colposcopy of the vaginal walls, and biopsy. Treatment: primary radiotherapy (brachytherapy with or without external beam radiotherapy) is the main treatment for most vaginal cancers, as surgery in the vagina typically requires radical excision that significantly impacts function. Early-stage small lesions may be amenable to surgical excision.

what is lichen sclerosus: Lichen sclerosus (LS) is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory skin condition that most commonly affects the vulva and perianal area in women (it can also occur extragenitally and in men). It is estimated to affect 1 in 70 to 100 women, with peaks in prepubertal girls and postmenopausal women (though it affects all ages). Symptoms: intense itch (pruritus vulvae), soreness, pain with sex, skin fragility (easy splitting, fissuring, bleeding from minor trauma), and difficulty with penetration due to introital narrowing. Signs: white, crinkled (cigarette-paper) skin; architectural change — progressive loss of the labia minora (resorption), clitoral hood phimosis, and introital narrowing in severe untreated disease. Pathological: an autoimmune condition associated with other autoimmune diseases (thyroid disease, vitiligo, alopecia areata). Not caused by infection. Treatment: potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment) — initial intensive course then maintenance, applied regularly to prevent progression and reduce the 4 to 5% lifetime cancer risk. Requires long-term follow-up.

what is lichen planus of the vulva: Vulval lichen planus (LP) is a chronic inflammatory mucocutaneous condition that causes erosive, painful lesions of the vulva and vagina. It is distinct from lichen sclerosus and typically more difficult to treat. Three vulvovaginal presentations: erosive (most common vulvovaginal form — raw, painful erythematous erosions with a white lacy border, typically affecting the vestibule and vaginal introitus); hypertrophic (raised thickened plaques); and classical (violaceous papules). Erosive vulvovaginal LP causes severe dyspareunia, postcoital bleeding, and vaginal discharge — scarring can lead to vaginal stenosis and complete obliteration in severe cases. It is associated with vulvovaginal-gingival (VVG) syndrome when it also affects the mouth and gums. Treatment: first-line is potent topical corticosteroids (betamethasone or clobetasol vaginal ointment or suppositories); tacrolimus 0.1% ointment for steroid-resistant or steroid-sparing use; systemic immunosuppression (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, ciclosporin) for severe erosive disease. Requires specialist vulvology or combined vulvology-dermatology clinic management.

what is the effect of tamoxifen on the m: Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used in the treatment and prevention of hormone receptor-positive breast cancer. It has anti-estrogenic effects on breast tissue but pro-estrogenic effects on the uterus and bone. Menstrual effects in pre-menopausal women: tamoxifen commonly causes menstrual irregularity — changes in cycle length, flow, and sometimes oligomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea. It does not cause menopause. Uterine effects: tamoxifen's estrogenic action on the endometrium causes endometrial thickening, polyp formation, hyperplasia, and — with long-term use — increased risk of endometrial cancer (approximately 2 to 3 times the background risk). Annual pelvic surveillance was previously recommended, but current evidence (and NICE guidelines) does not support routine annual transvaginal ultrasound in asymptomatic women on tamoxifen because the false-positive rate (tamoxifen causes subendometrial stromal changes that mimic thickening) leads to unnecessary procedures. Instead, any abnormal bleeding in women on tamoxifen must be urgently investigated. Pre-menopausal women on tamoxifen still need contraception — the drug can cause ovulatory dysfunction without eliminating ovulation.

what is menstrual leave and which countr: Menstrual leave (also called period leave) is a policy allowing people who menstruate to take time off work or school during painful or heavy periods without using standard sick leave. Japan has had statutory menstrual leave ('seirikyu') since 1947, though uptake is low due to stigma. South Korea, Indonesia, Taiwan, and Zambia have statutory provisions.
