Spain became the first European country to introduce paid menstrual leave (up to 3 days per month for severe dysmenorrhea, requiring a medical certificate) in 2023. Australia, the UK, and many other countries do not have statutory menstrual leave. The policy is debated: proponents argue it normalises menstrual health as a legitimate health issue and reduces presenteeism (working while unwell, which reduces productivity and worsens health). Critics raise concerns about stigma, potential discrimination against people who menstruate in hiring decisions, and the risk of medicalising normal menstruation. A number of UK companies (eg. Bupa, Vodafone, Unilever) have voluntary menstrual leave policies.

what is productivity across the menstrua: Cycle syncing is the practice of adapting work tasks, exercise, social activities, and nutrition to each phase of the menstrual cycle based on how hormonal changes affect energy, cognition, and mood. The concept was popularised by Alisa Vitti and has a basis in endocrinology, though the evidence for specific productivity optimisation by cycle phase is largely observational. Hormonal impacts on cognition and energy: follicular phase (rising estrogen) — rising energy, improved working memory, verbal fluency, and motivation from dopaminergic estrogen effects; ovulatory phase — peak energy, sociability, and confidence from LH and estrogen peak; luteal phase (progesterone dominance) — better attention to detail, methodical focus, and inward processing; some cognitive slowing; premenstrual phase (hormone withdrawal) — potential brain fog, reduced working memory, lower mood, which may impair complex task performance. Some research supports cycle-phase differences in cognitive performance, but individual variability is high and cycle regularity varies significantly. Tracking your own cycle to identify personal patterns is more practically useful than applying population-level averages.

what is polycystic ovary syndrome and in: Insulin resistance is central to PCOS pathophysiology in the majority of women with the condition — particularly those with the overweight or obese phenotype, though lean women with PCOS can also have insulin resistance. Insulin resistance causes compensatory hyperinsulinaemia, which drives: ovarian androgen overproduction (insulin stimulates theca cell androgen synthesis and reduces SHBG, increasing free testosterone), disrupted follicle development, and anovulation. Lifestyle intervention addressing insulin resistance is first-line PCOS treatment: combined dietary modification (reducing refined carbohydrate and glycaemic load, Mediterranean-style diet or low-glycaemic diet) with regular aerobic and resistance exercise improves insulin sensitivity, reduces hyperinsulinaemia, and restores menstrual regularity and ovulation in approximately 50 to 80% of overweight women with PCOS. Metformin: an insulin sensitiser that reduces hepatic glucose production and improves peripheral insulin sensitivity — improves menstrual regularity, reduces androgen levels, and improves ovulation in PCOS. Inositol (particularly myo-inositol and D-chiro-inositol): a dietary supplement shown in RCTs to improve insulin sensitivity and menstrual regularity in PCOS, with a good safety profile.

what are the different types of HRT estr: Estrogen can be delivered via multiple routes, each with different pharmacokinetics and risk profiles. Oral tablets (eg. Elleste Solo, Zumenon): undergo first-pass liver metabolism, which increases clotting factor production and may raise VTE (blood clot) and stroke risk; also increases triglycerides; most studied formulation but increasingly less preferred. Transdermal patches (eg. Evorel, Estradot, Fematrix): estradiol absorbed directly through the skin, bypassing liver first-pass — does not increase VTE risk; applied to abdomen, buttock, or thigh twice weekly or weekly depending on formulation. Transdermal gel (Oestrogel, Sandrena): estradiol rubbed onto skin daily; same advantages as patches (no VTE risk), highly flexible dosing, and particularly suitable for women with skin reactions to patch adhesive. Transdermal spray (Lenzetto): metered-dose estradiol spray applied to inner forearm. Vaginal estrogen (Vagifem tablets, Estring ring, Gina 10mcg — available OTC in UK from 2023, Ovestin cream): local low-dose estrogen for GSM only — minimal systemic absorption, safe even in women with breast cancer history (for most products) as confirmed by NICE guidance in 2023.

what is micronised progesterone and how: Micronised progesterone (Utrogestan in the UK) is a body-identical progesterone — the same molecular structure as the hormone produced naturally by the corpus luteum. It is micronised (reduced to microscopic particles) to improve absorption. Taken orally at bedtime (200 mg for 12 nights per cycle in sequential HRT, or 100 mg continuously in continuous combined HRT), or vaginally. Key differences from synthetic progestogens (norethisterone, levonorgestrel, medroxyprogesterone acetate — MPA): micronised progesterone does not have the same androgenic and glucocorticoid receptor binding that synthetic progestogens possess; does not worsen lipid profiles; does not counteract the beneficial cardiovascular effects of estrogen as MPA does; and appears to have a lower breast cancer risk signal. The E3N French cohort study and other observational data suggest combined estradiol and micronised progesterone (Utrogestan) has the lowest breast cancer risk of any combined HRT regimen. Micronised progesterone's main clinical advantage is that it has a sedative effect (via its conversion to allopregnanolone, a GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulator) — taken at bedtime, it improves sleep quality.

what is the non-hormonal management of m: For women who cannot or prefer not to use HRT, several non-hormonal approaches are evidence-based for menopausal symptoms. Vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes, night sweats): fezolinetant (NK3 receptor antagonist — new licensed option), SSRIs and SNRIs (venlafaxine 75 mg is most evidence-based, paroxetine, escitalopram — reduce flush frequency by approximately 50 to 60% vs 75 to 80% for HRT), gabapentin (off-label, effective particularly for night-time flushes), clonidine (alpha-2 agonist — modest evidence, significant side effects limit use). Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT): NICE-recommended, significantly reduces the distress and impact of menopausal symptoms even where vasomotor symptom frequency is not reduced — addresses hot flush-related anxiety and sleep disruption. Lifestyle: reducing alcohol and caffeine (vasodilatory triggers), layered clothing, keeping sleeping environment cool, smoking cessation. For vaginal dryness: non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers (Replens, YES WB) used regularly (not just before sex) and lubricants during sexual activity. Pelvic floor physiotherapy for urinary and sexual symptoms.

what is the genitourinary syndrome of me: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM) — formerly called vulvovaginal atrophy — is a broad syndrome encompassing the genital, sexual, and urinary symptoms that arise from estrogen deficiency at menopause. It affects approximately 40 to 50% of postmenopausal women and, unlike vasomotor symptoms which often improve with time, GSM typically worsens progressively without treatment. Genital symptoms: vaginal dryness, burning, irritation, soreness, and loss of vaginal rugae and elasticity. Sexual symptoms: dyspareunia (pain during sex), decreased lubrication, reduced arousal and sensation, and post-coital bleeding (from fragile vaginal walls). Urinary symptoms: urinary urgency and frequency, recurrent UTIs, urinary incontinence, and urethral discomfort. Diagnosis is clinical. Treatment: vaginal estrogen (first-line — Vagifem tablets, Estring ring, Ovestin cream, or Gina — safe for almost all women, including most with a history of breast cancer as per updated NICE 2023 guidance); ospemifene (oral SERM alternative); vaginal DHEA (prasterone). Non-hormonal: regular vaginal moisturisers (3 times weekly minimum) and lubricants during intercourse. Sexual activity and regular orgasm maintain blood flow and tissue health.

what is the effect of diet on the menstr: Diet profoundly influences the menstrual cycle through its effects on body composition, insulin sensitivity, inflammatory burden, and micronutrient status. Key dietary influences: glycaemic load — high-glycaemic diets worsen insulin resistance and amplify hyperandrogenism in PCOS-prone individuals, disrupting ovulation; anti-inflammatory diets (Mediterranean-style — rich in omega-3 fatty acids, colourful vegetables, legumes, olive oil) reduce prostaglandin E2 and leukotriene production, reducing dysmenorrhea severity; iron intake — low iron (from low red meat intake, vegan diet, or high blood loss) causes iron deficiency that impairs energy, exercise capacity, immune function, and cognitive performance; zinc is required for progesterone synthesis and immune function (pumpkin seeds, meat, legumes); magnesium deficiency worsens PMS symptoms and is found in approximately 50% of women; B vitamins (B6, B12, folate) support serotonin synthesis and reduce homocysteine; omega-3 fatty acids (EPA, DHA) directly compete with arachidonic acid for cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing pro-inflammatory prostaglandin production and decreasing period pain. Caloric restriction below energy availability threshold (as in relative energy deficiency in sport — RED-S) suppresses the HPG axis and causes hypothalamic amenorrhoea.

what is relative energy deficiency in sp: Relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S), formerly called the Female Athlete Triad, describes the syndrome of insufficient dietary energy intake to meet the demands of both exercise and normal physiological function. The original Female Athlete Triad described three interrelated conditions: low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction (ranging from subtle luteal phase deficiency to full hypothalamic amenorrhoea), and low bone density. RED-S expanded this framework to recognise that energy deficiency affects multiple body systems in both female and male athletes (though the menstrual/reproductive consequences are unique to females). The mechanism: insufficient calorie intake signals energy scarcity to the hypothalamus, which reduces GnRH pulsatility — suppressing LH, FSH, estrogen, and progesterone. Consequences for women: irregular or absent periods, reduced bone density (estrogen is critical for bone health), impaired immunity, reduced iron absorption, impaired muscle repair, and psychological effects (commonly comorbid with disordered eating). Risk groups: endurance athletes, aesthetic sport athletes (gymnastics, figure skating, ballet), weight-class sport athletes. Treatment centres on increasing energy availability — adding 300 to 500 calories daily and often reducing training load.

what is vaginismus: Vaginismus is involuntary contraction of the pelvic floor muscles around the vaginal opening in response to attempted penetration — including sexual intercourse, tampon insertion, speculum examination, or gynaecological procedures. It can make penetration impossible or extremely painful. Two categories: primary vaginismus (present from the first attempt at penetration, often lifelong) and secondary vaginismus (developing after a period of normal penetrative sex, often following a specific trigger — trauma, illness, relationship change, childbirth, menopause). It is involuntary — people with vaginismus typically want to have penetrative sex but cannot control the muscle response. Contributing factors include anxiety, past sexual trauma, negative sexual beliefs, genital pain conditions (vulvodynia, lichen sclerosus, GSM), and relationship factors. Treatment: highly effective — first-line is a combination of psychosexual therapy (addressing the psychological dimension), pelvic floor physiotherapy (graduated relaxation and progressive vaginal dilator use — starting with the smallest size and increasing gradually), and couples therapy where relevant. Vaginismus is curable in the majority of cases with appropriate treatment.

what is vulvodynia: Vulvodynia is chronic vulvar pain lasting 3 months or more without an identifiable underlying dermatological or infectious cause. It is classified by location (generalised — whole vulva, or localised — typically vestibulodynia, pain at the vestibule/vaginal entrance), provoked (by touch or pressure — the most common form) or unprovoked, and primary (present from the onset of sexual activity) or secondary. Provoked vestibulodynia (PVD) — pain at the vaginal opening triggered by touch, tampon insertion, or penetration — is the most common form and a major cause of dyspareunia. Causes are multifactorial and still being elucidated: altered peripheral nerve density in the vestibular tissue, central sensitisation (lowered pain threshold in the CNS), pelvic floor muscle hypertonicity (frequently coexists), hormonal factors (low-dose OCP use has been associated with vulvar vestibule changes reducing arousal and increasing vestibular sensitivity), and psychological factors (pain catastrophising, anxiety). Treatment: pelvic floor physiotherapy, topical lidocaine (desensitisation), low-dose tricyclic antidepressants or gabapentinoids (neuropathic pain modulation), CBT for pain catastrophising, and vestibulectomy (surgical removal of the vestibular tissue) in refractory provoked vestibulodynia — with approximately 75 to 90% success rate.

what is gender affirming surgery and its: Gender affirming (gender confirmation) surgery for transgender and non-binary people encompasses a range of procedures. For trans women (assigned male at birth): genital surgery (vaginoplasty — construction of a vagina and vulva) removes testosterone-producing testicular tissue — subsequent estrogen therapy is required. Fertility should be discussed before genital surgery because sperm preservation is possible pre-operatively. For trans men (assigned female at birth): hysterectomy (with or without oophorectomy) may be sought to achieve amenorrhoea and reduce dysphoria — oophorectomy removes the ovaries, eliminating the main source of estrogen (exogenous testosterone then provides adequate estrogen-like effects on bone). Fertility preservation (egg or embryo freezing) before any surgical procedures should be offered and discussed with all trans individuals who may wish to have biologically related children in the future. Some trans men choose not to have genital surgery but opt for hysterectomy alone (for amenorrhoea) or oophorectomy (to reduce residual estrogen). Post-hysterectomy, testosterone-treated trans men require lifelong monitoring of bone density if oophorectomy has been performed.

what is the history of menstrual taboo: Menstrual taboos — social, religious, and cultural restrictions on menstruating people — exist across cultures and throughout history, reflecting the universal significance societies have attributed to menstruation. In ancient Greece, Pliny the Elder described menstrual blood as capable of killing plants, souring wine, and rusting metal. Many ancient cultures (Hinduism, Judaism, Islam, various indigenous traditions) include varying degrees of menstrual pollution beliefs, from restrictions on preparing food and entering sacred spaces to physical separation from community. Victorian biomedicine pathologised menstruation — physicians described it as debilitating, arguing against women's education and employment on the grounds that cognitive work during menstruation caused reproductive harm. Anthropological research (e.g., Buckley and Gottlieb, 1988) argues that menstrual taboos are not uniformly negative — some function as protections for menstruating people, conferring spiritual power or communal status. The contemporary period poverty movement and menstrual health advocacy work to dismantle shame, improve education, and ensure access to period products — recognising that menstrual stigma drives health-seeking delays and perpetuates inequity.

what is period poverty: Period poverty is the lack of adequate access to menstrual products, hygiene facilities, education, and/or waste management related to menstruation — due to financial constraints, discrimination, or structural inequity. It is a global public health problem affecting people across income levels and countries. Consequences: product insecurity forces improvisation with unsafe alternatives (toilet paper, cloth, plastic bags), leading to health risks (toxic shock syndrome risk with prolonged improvised tampon use; infection risk from non-sterile materials). In schools and workplaces, product insecurity leads to absenteeism — girls in LMICs who cannot manage their periods may miss school or drop out entirely. In the UK, period poverty has been recognised as a significant issue — the Scottish Government in 2018 became the first to legislate for free period products in public buildings, schools, and universities; the Period Products (Free Provision) (Scotland) Act 2021 extended this to all public bodies. England introduced free period products in schools from 2020. VAT on period products ('tampon tax') was removed in the UK in January 2021 (previously 5%).

what is the importance of period trackin: Period tracking — recording the dates, duration, flow volume, and associated symptoms of each menstrual cycle — provides clinically useful data for both the individual and their healthcare providers. Tracking enables: early detection of cycle irregularity (changes in cycle length, missed periods, or unusual breakthrough bleeding that may indicate thyroid disease, PCOS, pregnancy, perimenopause, or endometrial pathology); identification of cyclical patterns in symptoms (migraines, mood changes, energy, pain, bloating — enabling cycle-informed management); monitoring response to treatment (whether a medication or lifestyle change has improved cycle regularity or symptom burden); fertility awareness (identifying fertile window for conception or to avoid conception when used as a method — see FABM entries); and identification of abnormal bleeding patterns requiring investigation. Digital period tracking apps (Clue, Flo, Natural Cycles) provide population-level epidemiological data — Clue is a research partner for academic studies. Raw paper-based tracking (period diary) is equally valid clinically.

what is menstruation and physical exerci: Exercise and menstruation are fully compatible for the vast majority of people. Current evidence does not support resting during menstruation — in fact, moderate physical activity during menstruation is beneficial and may reduce period pain (exercise promotes endorphin release, improves blood flow to the uterus, reduces prostaglandin sensitivity, and reduces psychological symptoms). Athletic performance varies across the cycle: the follicular phase (day 1 to 14, rising estrogen) is associated with higher pain tolerance, muscle recovery capacity, and aerobic performance; the late follicular-ovulatory phase (around day 12 to 16) is performance peak for many athletes. The luteal phase (rising progesterone, elevated core temperature by 0.3 to 0.5°C) impairs thermoregulation slightly, which may reduce heat tolerance during sustained exertion. Energy availability is lower premenstrually for some people. Some elite athletes train and periodise their training cycles with their menstrual cycle — this is an emerging and individualised area. Practical considerations during menstruation: menstrual cups and period underwear allow uninterrupted swimming and water sports.

what is the relationship between sleep a: Sleep quality and architecture are significantly affected by menstrual cycle hormones. In the luteal phase, progesterone (which is sedative via its metabolite allopregnanolone, a GABA-A receptor modulator) increases subjective sleepiness and can increase slow-wave sleep. However, the elevated basal body temperature in the luteal phase — 0.3 to 0.5°C above follicular temperature — has a paradoxical effect on sleep quality: while warmth induces drowsiness, maintaining a cooler core body temperature is required for sustained deep sleep, so the luteal temperature elevation may impair sleep quality and cause more nocturnal awakenings. Premenstrually, as progesterone and estrogen fall, both sleep onset difficulty and sleep continuity worsen — this is most pronounced in those with PMDD. Menstrual pain also disrupts sleep in the first 1 to 2 days of the period. REM sleep quantity is associated with estrogen levels — rising estrogen in the follicular phase may increase REM sleep. Practically: maintaining consistent sleep schedules, ensuring bedroom temperature is low (18 to 20°C), and treating premenstrual insomnia with relaxation techniques are helpful. Magnesium glycinate (300 to 400 mg before bed) has some evidence for improving sleep quality premenstrually.

what is the gut-hormone axis and menstru: The gut microbiome influences estrogen metabolism through the estrobolome — the collection of gut bacteria capable of metabolising estrogens. In healthy gut function, estrogen conjugates (inactivated estrogens destined for excretion) are deconjugated by bacterial beta-glucuronidase enzymes back into active estrogens, which are reabsorbed into the circulation (enterohepatic recirculation). Dysbiosis (unhealthy gut microbiome) with elevated beta-glucuronidase activity increases estrogen recirculation, potentially contributing to estrogen excess conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and estrogen-driven cancers. Conversely, gut dysbiosis can also impair estrogen metabolism, causing relative estrogen deficiency. The gut-hormone axis also operates in reverse: estrogen influences gut motility, intestinal barrier function, and gut microbiome composition — explaining why gastrointestinal symptoms (bloating, diarrhoea, constipation) are common perimenstrually. High-fibre diets support healthy estrogen clearance by increasing gut transit (reducing reabsorption time), while supporting Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species that maintain low beta-glucuronidase activity.

what is coeliac disease and its effects: Coeliac disease is an autoimmune condition triggered by gluten ingestion in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DQ2 or DQ8), causing small intestinal inflammation and villous atrophy that impairs nutrient absorption. Reproductive manifestations of coeliac disease (before or with ongoing gluten exposure) include: delayed menarche (malabsorption of nutrients necessary for HPG axis maturation); menstrual irregularity and amenorrhoea from undernutrition and iron, zinc, and B vitamin deficiency; recurrent miscarriage (associated with anti-transglutaminase and anti-endomysial antibodies); reduced fertility and impaired pregnancy outcomes; early menopause; and elevated risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. In diagnosed coeliac disease, a strict gluten-free diet — allowing full intestinal healing and nutrient absorption restoration — normalises most of these reproductive consequences, including restoring menstrual regularity and improving fertility. All women with unexplained infertility, recurrent miscarriage, or menstrual irregularity without another clear cause should be tested for coeliac disease (tissue transglutaminase IgA with total IgA).

what is inflammatory bowel disease and i: Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis (inflammatory bowel disease, IBD) frequently affect women of reproductive age and interact with the menstrual cycle in both directions. IBD affects the cycle: active inflammation and associated malnutrition, iron deficiency anaemia, and chronic steroid use can cause menstrual irregularity, oligomenorrhoea, and impaired fertility. IBD flares are common in the premenstrual and menstrual phases — prostaglandins released during menstruation contribute to intestinal dysmotility and inflammation. Women with IBD frequently report worse bowel symptoms (urgency, diarrhoea, cramping) during menstruation, which can be difficult to distinguish from disease flare versus normal menstrual bowel changes. The cycle affects IBD: estrogen and progesterone modulate intestinal motility and gut barrier integrity — cyclical hormonal changes directly affect IBD symptom patterns. Contraception in IBD: the combined oral contraceptive pill is associated with a modestly increased risk of IBD exacerbation and VTE in active disease — progesterone-only options or non-hormonal contraception are preferred in active IBD. Many IBD medications (methotrexate, mycophenolate) are teratogenic — effective contraception and pre-conception planning are essential.

what is sickle cell disease and its effe: Sickle cell disease (SCD) — including HbSS (sickle cell anaemia), HbSC, and HbS-beta thalassaemia — has significant interactions with the menstrual cycle and reproductive health. Menstrual effects: SCD causes chronic haemolytic anaemia, exacerbated by menstrual blood loss — heavy periods significantly worsen anaemia and can trigger vasoocclusive crises (sickling occurs more readily in anaemic and hypoxic states). Women with SCD are more likely to have delayed menarche and menstrual irregularity. The combined oral contraceptive pill contains estrogen, which increases VTE risk — already elevated in SCD — making it relatively contraindicated. Preferred contraception in SCD: progesterone-only options (Depo-Provera injection — also reduces VTE risk and suppresses menstruation, beneficially reducing blood loss; Nexplanon implant; progesterone-only pill; Mirena IUS). Hydroxyurea (a disease-modifying treatment for SCD) is teratogenic — effective contraception is essential in women of reproductive potential taking it. Pregnancy in SCD is high-risk, requiring specialist joint obstetric-haematology care (increased risk of VTE, preterm birth, pre-eclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and maternal mortality).

what is rheumatoid arthritis and its int: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune inflammatory arthritis that disproportionately affects women (3:1 ratio), typically presenting in the reproductive years or at menopause. RA symptoms cyclically track hormonal changes. Many women with RA report worsening joint pain, stiffness, and fatigue premenstrually (when estrogen and progesterone fall, removing their anti-inflammatory effects). Conversely, the majority of women with RA experience significant improvement in disease activity during pregnancy — estrogen and particularly progesterone shift the immune response toward an anti-inflammatory Th2 profile, partially suppressing the Th17-driven autoimmunity underlying RA. After delivery, RA frequently flares (sometimes presenting for the first time postpartum) as progesterone withdrawal unmasks or amplifies autoimmune activity. RA medications and contraception/fertility: methotrexate requires effective contraception (teratogenic — discontinue 3 months before conception); leflunomide is teratogenic and requires washout; biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors) are generally safe in the first and second trimester but usually stopped in the third. Prednisolone and hydroxychloroquine are safe in pregnancy.

what is multiple sclerosis and the menst: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune demyelinating condition of the central nervous system that predominantly affects women (3:1 ratio) and typically presents between ages 20 and 40. Approximately 35 to 54% of women with MS report symptom worsening premenstrually and during menstruation — the phenomenon is sometimes called 'catamenial MS' by analogy with catamenial epilepsy. Mechanisms: progesterone's metabolite allopregnanolone has neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory effects — its premenstrual withdrawal may transiently worsen symptoms. Estrogen is generally neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory; its premenstrual decline may also contribute. Symptoms typically affected: fatigue (already the most common MS symptom), cognitive difficulties (brain fog), spasticity, bladder urgency, and depression — all commonly worsen premenstrually. During pregnancy, relapse rate falls dramatically in the second and third trimesters (particularly with high estrogen/progesterone); postpartum rebound increase in relapse rate (first 3 to 6 months) is well-established and should be anticipated in management planning, often requiring prompt reinitiation of disease-modifying therapy.

what is the relationship between lupus (: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a systemic autoimmune condition that affects women approximately 9 times more than men, strongly implicating estrogen in its pathogenesis. Estrogen is pro-inflammatory and promotes B cell hyperreactivity — the characteristic autoimmune mechanism of SLE. SLE and the menstrual cycle: lupus flares are common premenstrually and during menstruation — the estrogen spike in the follicular phase and around ovulation may also trigger flares. Many women with SLE experience worsening arthralgia, fatigue, skin rashes, and nephritis activity cyclically. Contraception considerations: estrogen-containing contraceptives are relatively or absolutely contraindicated in SLE (particularly in those with antiphospholipid antibodies — significantly elevated VTE risk; or with active nephritis). Progesterone-only methods are preferred. Pregnancy in SLE requires pre-conception disease control (ideally ≥6 months of quiescence), specialist obstetric care, monitoring for maternal complications (nephritis flare, pre-eclampsia), and neonatal lupus monitoring if anti-Ro/La antibodies are present (can cause congenital heart block).

what is antiphospholipid syndrome and ho: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is an autoimmune condition characterised by persistently elevated antiphospholipid antibodies (anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I, or lupus anticoagulant) associated with thrombosis (venous or arterial) and/or pregnancy morbidity. Pregnancy manifestations of APS: recurrent miscarriage (three or more losses before 10 weeks, or one or more losses after 10 weeks, or one or more premature births before 34 weeks from severe pre-eclampsia or IUGR). APS is the most common treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage. Mechanism: antiphospholipid antibodies impair trophoblast invasion, placental function, and promote intraplacental thrombosis. Treatment during pregnancy: low-dose aspirin (75 to 150 mg daily, started preconceptionally) plus low molecular weight heparin (LMWH — from a positive pregnancy test) dramatically improves live birth rates (from approximately 20% without treatment to approximately 70 to 80% with aspirin plus LMWH). All women with recurrent miscarriage (two or more losses) should be tested for antiphospholipid antibodies.

what is thrombophilia and its effect on: Inherited thrombophilias are genetic conditions that increase the tendency to form blood clots. In the context of reproductive health: they are associated with increased VTE (venous thromboembolism) risk during pregnancy and the puerperium (postpartum), and have been studied as causes of recurrent miscarriage, placental insufficiency, IUGR, and stillbirth. Most common inherited thrombophilias: factor V Leiden mutation (most common — present in approximately 5% of white European population, associated with 4 to 8 times elevated VTE risk); prothrombin gene mutation (G20210A); protein C, S, or antithrombin deficiency; MTHFR C677T polymorphism (associated with elevated homocysteine — folic acid supplementation mitigates this). The association between inherited thrombophilias and recurrent miscarriage remains controversial — evidence is weaker than for APS, and routine screening and treatment of inherited thrombophilias for recurrent miscarriage is not currently recommended in RCOG or ESHRE guidelines. However, testing is appropriate in the context of late pregnancy losses (after 14 weeks) or severe placental complications.

what is the connection between vitamin D: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble hormone (not truly a vitamin) produced in the skin by UV-B radiation acting on 7-dehydrocholesterol, with receptors expressed throughout the reproductive tract. Evidence for vitamin D in menstrual health: low vitamin D is associated with increased period pain (dysmenorrhea) — vitamin D reduces prostaglandin synthesis and has direct anti-inflammatory effects; vitamin D supplementation (50,000 IU weekly for 8 weeks) reduced dysmenorrhea pain severity in RCTs. PCOS: vitamin D deficiency is highly prevalent in PCOS and is associated with greater insulin resistance, worse metabolic parameters, and reduced AMH levels — supplementation modestly improves insulin sensitivity and menstrual regularity. Endometriosis: lower vitamin D levels are found in women with endometriosis; vitamin D reduces endometrial cell proliferation and inflammation in vitro, but RCT evidence in endometriosis is limited. Fertility: vitamin D receptors are expressed in granulosa cells and the endometrium — low vitamin D is associated with poorer IVF outcomes in observational studies, and deficiency correction is recommended in women undergoing IVF. The UK recommends 10 mcg (400 IU) vitamin D daily for all adults, year-round for those with limited sun exposure.

what are the symptoms of ovarian cancer: Ovarian cancer is called 'the silent killer' because its symptoms are vague and non-specific, often attributed to other causes, causing most women to present with advanced-stage disease (Stage III or IV — approximately 75% of diagnoses). UK incidence: approximately 7,500 new cases per year. Symptoms that should trigger assessment: persistent bloating (not just bloating that comes and goes), quickly feeling full when eating, pelvic or abdominal pain, and urinary symptoms (urgency or frequency) — particularly when these occur more than 12 times per month. Other symptoms: change in bowel habits, unexplained fatigue, back pain, abnormal vaginal bleeding. NICE guidelines (2011, updated 2023) state that women (especially over 50) experiencing these symptoms repeatedly and persistently should have CA125 measured. A raised CA125 (>35 U/ml) prompts pelvic ultrasound. CA125 is not a screening test (insufficient sensitivity and specificity), but the UK Collaborative Trial of Ovarian Cancer Screening (UKCTOCS) showed no mortality benefit from population screening. Risk factors: age (peak 75 to 79), BRCA1/2 mutations (dramatically elevated risk), Lynch syndrome, family history, nulliparity, endometriosis (clear cell and endometrioid subtypes). Protective: OCP use (reduces risk by approximately 50% with 5+ years use), breastfeeding, tubal ligation.

what is the impact of smoking on menstru: Smoking has multiple deleterious effects on the menstrual cycle, fertility, and reproductive ageing. Chemicals in cigarette smoke (particularly polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, cadmium, and nicotine) are directly ovotoxic — they accelerate follicle depletion (primordial follicle apoptosis), reducing ovarian reserve. Smokers reach menopause approximately 1 to 2 years earlier than non-smokers. Smoking impairs the HPG axis, disrupting LH pulsatility and potentially interfering with the LH surge required for ovulation. Heavy smokers have more irregular cycles. Fertility: smokers take longer to conceive; smoking reduces IVF success rates (egg quality and embryo development are impaired); and the ectopic pregnancy rate is higher in smokers (smoking impairs tubal ciliary function). Dysmenorrhea is worse in smokers (nicotine causes vasoconstriction, reducing uterine blood flow and impairing prostaglandin metabolism). Smoking during pregnancy causes placental insufficiency, IUGR, premature birth, stillbirth, and SIDS. Smoking cessation at any age improves reproductive outcomes — complete cessation is the most impactful intervention.

what is alcohol and its effect on the me: Alcohol consumption affects menstrual and reproductive health in a dose-dependent manner. Heavy alcohol use (>14 units per week) disrupts HPG axis function, causing: menstrual irregularity (oligomenorrhoea, amenorrhoea), anovulation, hyperprolactinaemia, and altered estrogen metabolism (the liver metabolises both alcohol and estrogen — heavy alcohol use impairs estrogen clearance, raising circulating estrogen). Alcohol also impairs progesterone production in the luteal phase. Fertility: women consuming ≥14 units per week have a significantly longer time to conception and lower IVF success rates. In IVF, even moderate alcohol consumption in the cycle preceding retrieval is associated with fewer eggs retrieved and lower live birth rates. In pregnancy: no safe level of alcohol in pregnancy is established. Alcohol crosses the placenta and causes fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD) — a range of neurodevelopmental, physical, and behavioural problems. All major health bodies (WHO, NICE, RCOG) recommend complete alcohol abstinence during pregnancy and while trying to conceive. Light to moderate drinking (≤7 units per week) has modest if any measurable reproductive effects in most studies, but no safe threshold is defined.

what is the connection between thyroid d: The thyroid and reproductive systems are tightly interconnected. Thyroid hormone is required for normal follicle development, ovulation, implantation, and pregnancy maintenance. Thyroid disorders are among the most common causes of menstrual irregularity. Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid — low T4, elevated TSH): causes heavy periods (menorrhagia — thyroid hormone deficiency impairs clotting factor production), irregular periods, oligomenorrhoea, anovulation, and in severe cases amenorrhoea; it also raises prolactin (via elevated TRH from the hypothalamus), causing galactorrhoea and further cycle disruption; reduced fertility and early pregnancy loss. Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid — elevated T4, suppressed TSH): causes lighter and less frequent periods (oligomenorrhoea), shortened cycles, and amenorrhoea in severe cases; also impairs fertility. In pregnancy: thyroid demand increases significantly (the fetus is dependent on maternal thyroid hormone in the first trimester); pre-existing thyroid disease must be optimised before conception (TSH ideally <2.5 mIU/L); TPO antibody positivity (Hashimoto's thyroiditis) independently increases miscarriage risk even when TSH is normal.

what is prolactin and how does elevated: Prolactin is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland that primarily stimulates breast milk production during lactation. Elevated prolactin outside of breastfeeding (hyperprolactinaemia) disrupts menstrual function by suppressing GnRH pulsatility, reducing LH and FSH, and preventing follicle development and ovulation. Causes of hyperprolactinaemia: physiological (breastfeeding, pregnancy, nipple stimulation, stress, sleep, exercise — transient); medications (antipsychotics, antiemetics such as metoclopramide and domperidone, antidepressants, antihypertensives including methyldopa, verapamil); pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma — benign tumour of prolactin-secreting cells — the most common pituitary tumour); hypothyroidism (elevated TRH stimulates prolactin); macroprolactinaemia (biologically inactive prolactin complexes — common cause of elevated prolactin test without clinical symptoms); and chronic renal failure. Clinical features of hyperprolactinaemia: oligomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, anovulatory infertility, and reduced libido. Prolactinomas are treated with dopamine agonists (cabergoline — first-line, highly effective at normalising prolactin and shrinking the adenoma; bromocriptine is an alternative).

what is thyroid nodule and thyroid cance: Thyroid nodules are extremely common — detectable by ultrasound in approximately 19 to 67% of people, with increasing prevalence with age. They are approximately 4 times more common in women than men. The vast majority (>95%) are benign. Thyroid cancer affects approximately 4,000 people per year in the UK. Differentiated thyroid cancers (papillary — most common, accounts for 80%; follicular — 10%) are strongly associated with female sex and typically have an excellent prognosis (5-year survival >98%). Medullary thyroid cancer (associated with MEN2 syndrome) and anaplastic thyroid cancer (rare, aggressive) are distinct entities. Evaluation of a thyroid nodule: blood tests (TSH, T4), neck ultrasound (using standardised risk classification — TIRADS), and fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) for nodules with suspicious ultrasound features. Treatment of differentiated thyroid cancer: thyroidectomy (total or hemithyroidectomy), radioactive iodine ablation for intermediate- and high-risk disease, and lifelong thyroxine replacement. Pregnancy and thyroid cancer: differentiated thyroid cancer discovered in pregnancy can generally be safely managed with surgery deferred until after delivery if first trimester, or performed in the second trimester if needed.

what is the role of progesterone in recu: Progesterone is essential for maintaining early pregnancy: it suppresses uterine contractions, promotes endometrial decidualisation, modulates the local immune environment (reducing NK cell cytotoxicity, promoting Th2 tolerogenic response toward the implanting embryo), and supports placental development. In women with recurrent miscarriage, two large RCTs (PROMISE trial 2015 and PRISM trial 2019 — both UK-based) have informed clinical practice. PROMISE found no significant benefit of progesterone supplementation in women with unexplained recurrent miscarriage (defined as ≥3 losses) who had no evidence of low progesterone. PRISM found that in women with a history of recurrent miscarriage who presented with bleeding in early pregnancy, micronised vaginal progesterone (400 mg twice daily from presentation to 16 weeks) significantly increased live birth rates compared to placebo — a 5.4 percentage point improvement overall, and 8 to 15 percentage points in those with prior losses. Current RCOG guidance recommends considering progesterone support in women with recurrent miscarriage who present with early pregnancy bleeding, based on the PRISM data. Progesterone support (luteal phase support) is standard in IVF treatment.

what is ectopic pregnancy and how is it: An ectopic pregnancy is one that implants outside the uterine cavity — 95% in the fallopian tube, rarely in the cervix, ovary, interstitium, or on a caesarean scar. It affects approximately 1 in 90 pregnancies in the UK. Risk factors: prior tubal surgery (including sterilisation), previous ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease (tubal scarring), IVF (embryo transfer — small risk of tubal migration), IUD in situ (if conception occurs despite contraception), and smoking. Symptoms: typically at 5 to 8 weeks, before the diagnosis is sometimes expected — lower abdominal pain (often one-sided), light vaginal bleeding, shoulder tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation from haemoperitoneum), and if ruptured: sudden severe pain, collapse, and haemodynamic shock. Diagnosis: transvaginal ultrasound (empty uterus with or without adnexal mass) and serial bhCG (rising bhCG without intrauterine pregnancy is concerning). Management: expectant management (bhCG <1000 IU/L, asymptomatic, falling); medical management (methotrexate — single-dose IM injection — for unruptured ectopic with bhCG <3000 IU/L in some protocols, hemodynamically stable, no fetal cardiac activity); surgical management (laparoscopic salpingostomy — preserving the tube, or salpingectomy — removing it — the definitive treatment for ruptured or failed medical management).

what is recurrent miscarriage investigat: Recurrent miscarriage (RM) is defined by RCOG as three or more consecutive pregnancy losses before 24 weeks (in the UK; ESHRE now defines it as two or more). It affects approximately 1 in 100 couples. Investigation: antiphospholipid antibodies (anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2GP1, lupus anticoagulant) — the most important treatable cause; parental karyotyping (chromosomal abnormalities in the couple account for approximately 4 to 5% — most commonly balanced translocations); uterine anatomy assessment (hysteroscopy, 3D ultrasound, MRI — uterine anomalies, particularly septate uterus, associated with increased miscarriage risk); thyroid function and thyroid antibodies; HbA1c; FMR1 premutation. In approximately 50% of couples, no cause is identified. Evidence-based management: aspirin plus LMWH heparin for APS; hysteroscopic surgery for uterine septum; thyroid treatment; progesterone for early bleeding (PRISM evidence); referral to specialist recurrent miscarriage clinic (eg. Tommy's National Centre for Miscarriage Research). Supportive care with early pregnancy surveillance (USS from 6 weeks) in the next pregnancy significantly improves outcomes even without pharmacological treatment — a form of supportive 'tender loving care' has RCT evidence for improved live birth rates.

what is a retroverted uterus: A retroverted (tilted or tipped) uterus is one that tilts backward toward the rectum instead of forward toward the bladder. Approximately 20 to 25% of women have a retroverted uterus, making it a common anatomical variant rather than an abnormality. In most women, it causes no symptoms and is not a cause of infertility or pregnancy complications — the uterus typically moves forward (anteverts) in the second trimester as it grows. A retroverted uterus can cause deep dyspareunia in certain sexual positions (particularly rear-entry positions where the penis strikes the cervix), and more painful smear tests (the speculum angle differs). Symptoms can be minimised by changing sexual position (side-lying positions, or woman-on-top allowing depth control). A retroverted uterus can also be caused by endometriosis or pelvic adhesions — if it is fixed (non-mobile, i.e., it cannot be repositioned during examination) rather than freely mobile, investigation for endometriosis is warranted. A freely mobile retroverted uterus requires no treatment.

what is an endometrial polyp: An endometrial polyp is a localised overgrowth of endometrial tissue attached by a stalk (pedicle) to the inner wall of the uterus. Polyps range in size from a few millimetres to several centimetres. They are common (affecting approximately 3 to 5% of women undergoing fertility investigation) and most are benign. Symptoms: frequently asymptomatic and found incidentally on ultrasound; when symptomatic they cause intermenstrual bleeding (spotting between periods), heavy periods, post-coital bleeding, or postmenopausal bleeding. A postmenopausal polyp requires investigation because the risk of malignant or premalignant change is higher than in premenopausal polyps (approximately 5 to 10%). Risk factors: obesity, tamoxifen use, hypertension, chronic estrogen excess. Diagnosis: transvaginal ultrasound (a focal thickening or echogenic lesion within the endometrium); saline infusion sonography (SIS — fluid outlines the polyp clearly); or hysteroscopy (the gold standard — direct visualisation and surgical removal in the same procedure). Treatment: symptomatic polyps are removed by hysteroscopic polypectomy under local or general anaesthetic. Small asymptomatic premenopausal polyps may resolve spontaneously and can be observed.

what is a haemorrhagic ovarian cyst: A haemorrhagic ovarian cyst (also called a hemorrhagic corpus luteum) occurs when a blood vessel ruptures inside an ovarian cyst, causing bleeding into the cyst. It is most commonly a haemorrhagic corpus luteum — the corpus luteum (the progesterone-secreting structure that forms after ovulation) bleeds internally. This is a physiological event that is common and self-limiting in most cases. Symptoms: sudden onset sharp one-sided lower abdominal or pelvic pain, typically in the second half of the menstrual cycle (luteal phase), sometimes radiating to the back or thigh. Most resolve spontaneously within 6 to 8 weeks. Occasionally a ruptured haemorrhagic corpus luteum causes significant intraperitoneal bleeding, requiring hospitalisation and, rarely, surgical haemostasis. Ultrasound appearance: a characteristic lacy or web-like pattern within the cyst (reticular echogenicity from fibrin strands) is typical of haemorrhagic cysts. As the blood breaks down over weeks, the appearance changes. Follow-up ultrasound at 6 to 8 weeks confirms resolution. In women on anticoagulants, haemorrhagic corpus luteum cysts can be larger and cause more significant bleeding.

what is piriformis syndrome and pelvic p: Piriformis syndrome occurs when the piriformis muscle (a deep external hip rotator running from the sacrum to the greater trochanter of the femur, in close proximity to the sciatic nerve) becomes tight, inflamed, or in spasm, compressing or irritating the sciatic nerve. In women, the piriformis is often implicated in pelvic and hip pain that is cyclically modulated. Symptoms: buttock pain, deep gluteal pain, pain radiating down the back of the thigh and leg (sciatica), and pain worsening with sitting, walking, or climbing stairs. Pelvic pain relevance: endometriosis involving the posterior cul-de-sac or uterosacral ligaments can directly irritate the piriformis via inflammation and adhesion; pelvic floor hypertonicity (common in pelvic pain syndromes) often co-involves the piriformis. Piriformis is best assessed by a pelvic floor physiotherapist or musculoskeletal physiotherapist who can palpate it internally (through the vaginal wall) and assess range of hip movement. Treatment: stretching (piriformis stretch in supine hip flexion and external rotation), manual trigger point release, dry needling, and addressing contributing pelvic floor dysfunction.

what is neuropathic pelvic pain: Neuropathic pelvic pain arises from damage, sensitisation, or dysfunction of nerves in the pelvic region — the pudendal nerve, ilioinguinal nerve, iliohypogastric nerve, genitofemoral nerve, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, or nerve roots of the lumbosacral plexus. Neuropathic pain has characteristic qualities: burning, shooting, electric shock-like, stabbing, or tingling quality — distinct from the dull, aching visceral quality of dysmenorrhea or the sharp pain of musculoskeletal origin. Causes: childbirth (stretching and compression of the pudendal nerve during prolonged second stage), prior pelvic surgery (hysterectomy, caesarean section, inguinal hernia repair — mesh procedures particularly implicated), endometriosis (nerve infiltration by deep disease), and no identifiable cause in some cases. Diagnosis is clinical and requires careful neurological examination of the perineum, identification of trigger points, and response to diagnostic nerve blocks. Treatment: neuropathic medications (tricyclic antidepressants — amitriptyline 10 to 75 mg; gabapentin or pregabalin; SNRIs — duloxetine); pulsed radiofrequency of affected nerves; targeted nerve blocks; and pelvic floor physiotherapy for contributing muscular tension.

what is the normal range for menstrual c: A normal menstrual cycle length ranges from 24 to 38 days, measured from the first day of one period to the first day of the next period. This range was established by large-scale epidemiological studies including the SWAN study and Clue's dataset of millions of cycle records. The average cycle length in the general population is approximately 29 to 30 days, though 28 days (the textbook figure) is not the most common length and should not be taken as the only normal. Cycle-to-cycle variability of up to 7 to 9 days within the same person is considered normal. Cycles shorter than 24 days (polymenorrhoea) or longer than 38 days (oligomenorrhoea) warrant investigation. Cycle length is longest in adolescence (mean approximately 34 days in the year after menarche), gradually shortens through the 20s and 30s, and shortens further and becomes more irregular in the perimenopause. A single unusually long or short cycle in an otherwise regular person is generally not a cause for concern; consistent pattern changes over several cycles warrant evaluation.

what is the normal duration and volume o: A normal period lasts between 3 and 8 days. Total blood loss per period normally ranges from approximately 5 to 80 ml, with a mean of approximately 30 to 40 ml. Heavy menstrual bleeding is defined as >80 ml per cycle (previously quantified by a standardised alkaline haematin method, now assessed clinically as blood loss that impacts quality of life). Practically, 80 ml corresponds to soaking more than one regular pad or tampon per hour for several consecutive hours, passing large clots (greater than 50p-piece size), or bleeding through to clothing and bedding. Flow is typically heaviest on days 2 to 3. The colour of normal menstrual blood ranges from bright red (fresh blood, typically at its heaviest) to dark red or brown (older, slower-flowing blood, common at the start and end of the period). Small clots (smaller than a grape) are normal during heavier flow days. Periods lasting more than 8 days are considered prolonged.

what are menstrual clots and when are th: Menstrual clots form when blood pools in the uterus or vagina faster than anticoagulant factors in the menstrual fluid can break it down. Passing some small clots (grape-sized or smaller) during the heaviest days of the period is normal — clotting helps limit blood loss. Large clots (larger than a 50p piece or golf ball) indicate heavy flow that is outpacing the anticoagulant mechanism and are a sign of heavy menstrual bleeding that may warrant investigation and treatment. Heavy clotting is commonly associated with: uterine fibroids (particularly submucosal fibroids, which distort the uterine cavity and impair clotting), adenomyosis, endometrial polyps, and hypothyroidism. In early pregnancy, passing tissue-containing clots with pain may indicate miscarriage. If heavy clotting is accompanied by pallor, fatigue, breathlessness, or palpitations, iron deficiency anaemia from blood loss may have developed and haematological assessment is needed.

what is secondary amenorrhoea and how is: Secondary amenorrhoea is the absence of periods for 3 or more consecutive months in someone who has previously had regular periods (or 6 months in someone who previously had irregular periods). Common causes include: pregnancy (must always be excluded first); hypothalamic amenorrhoea (low energy availability, excessive exercise, psychological stress — GnRH suppression with low FSH, LH, estrogen, and normal prolactin); hyperprolactinaemia (elevated prolactin from any cause); hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism; PCOS (oligo/amenorrhoea from anovulation); premature ovarian insufficiency (elevated FSH); and anatomical causes (Asherman's syndrome — intrauterine adhesions from uterine surgery). Investigation: pregnancy test; FSH and LH (elevated in POI, suppressed in hypothalamic amenorrhoea, may be elevated or normal in PCOS); estradiol; prolactin; thyroid function (TSH, T4); testosterone and DHEA-S (if clinical hyperandrogenism); and progesterone challenge test (10 days of norethisterone — withdrawal bleed occurs if there is an estrogen-primed endometrium, indicating the cause is anovulation rather than structural; no bleed suggests structural cause or severe hypoestrogenism).

what is Asherman's syndrome: Asherman's syndrome (intrauterine adhesions or intrauterine synechiae) is the formation of scar tissue within the uterine cavity, most commonly following uterine surgery performed during or after pregnancy — particularly surgical evacuation of pregnancy (ERPC) for miscarriage or retained placental products, especially if performed when the uterus was infected. Other causes: endometrial ablation, hysteroscopic surgery, caesarean section, curettage for other reasons. The adhesions may partially or completely obliterate the uterine cavity, tethering the anterior and posterior walls together. Symptoms: reduced or absent periods (hypomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea) despite normal hormonal function — the endometrium cannot respond to hormones if it has been replaced by scar tissue; cyclical pelvic pain (from blood trapped behind adhesions — cryptomenorrhoea); recurrent miscarriage and infertility (the uterine cavity is necessary for implantation). Diagnosis: hysteroscopy (gold standard — direct visualisation and simultaneous treatment) or saline infusion sonography. Treatment: hysteroscopic lysis of adhesions with special scissors or energy devices, followed by intrauterine device or balloon placement and estrogen stimulation to promote endometrial regeneration.

what is Müllerian anomaly and how does i: Müllerian anomalies (also called congenital uterine anomalies) result from abnormal development, fusion, or resorption of the Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts, which form the uterus, fallopian tubes, cervix, and upper vagina during fetal development. They affect approximately 5 to 7% of the general population and 8 to 10% of women with recurrent miscarriage. The ESHRE/ESGE classification describes: U0 (normal uterus), U1 (dysmorphic — arcuate or T-shaped uterus), U2 (septate — most common, the midline septum fails to fully resorb, creating a fibrous partial or complete division of the cavity), U3 (bicorporeal — didelphys or bicornuate uterus, the ducts fail to fully fuse), U4 (hemi-uterus — one Müllerian duct develops), U5 (aplastic — absent uterus, as in MRKH syndrome). Menstrual implications: a septate uterus may cause cyclical pain from retrograde menstrual flow into a blind pouch in complete septate cases; non-communicating rudimentary uterine horn causes cryptomenorrhoea and progressive cyclical pain. Uterine didelphys typically causes two separate uterine cavities with two separate cervices — periods continue normally through both.

what is MRKH syndrome: Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome is a congenital condition in which the uterus and upper vagina fail to develop (uterovaginal aplasia), despite normal female (46,XX) chromosomes and normal secondary sexual development (breast development and pubic hair occur normally because the ovaries are intact). It affects approximately 1 in 4,000 to 5,000 female births and is typically diagnosed when menarche fails to occur (primary amenorrhoea) despite normal puberty. Women with MRKH have functional ovaries (so can achieve pregnancy via surrogacy using their own eggs or donor eggs), but have no uterus. A short vaginal dimple is usually present. Treatment focuses on vaginal lengthening to enable sexual intercourse: progressive self-dilation using vaginal dilators (the Frank method — progressive pressure applied daily, achieving adequate vaginal depth over 3 to 6 months in most women without surgery) is first-line. Surgical vaginoplasty (McIndoe procedure or Vecchietti technique) is offered when dilator therapy fails. Uterine transplantation, though experimental, has resulted in successful pregnancies in several women with MRKH and is now offered in specialist centres.

what is dyspareunia and how is it assess: Dyspareunia is pain during sexual intercourse or other forms of sexual penetration. It is classified by location: superficial dyspareunia (pain at the vaginal entrance — the vestibule) versus deep dyspareunia (internal pelvic pain triggered by deep penetration). Superficial causes: vaginismus, vulvodynia/vestibulodynia, genito-urinary syndrome of menopause (vaginal atrophy and dryness), vulvar skin conditions (lichen sclerosus, lichen planus), candidal infection, or inadequate arousal and lubrication. Deep causes: endometriosis (particularly involving the posterior cul-de-sac, uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum), adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, ovarian cysts, pelvic inflammatory disease, pelvic congestion syndrome, retroverted uterus (in some positions), and irritable bowel syndrome. Assessment: comprehensive sexual and gynaecological history (onset, timing relative to cycle, positions affected, associated symptoms), followed by gentle vulvar and vaginal examination and bimanual pelvic examination. A coordinated approach involving gynaecology, psychosexual therapy, pelvic floor physiotherapy, and where relevant, specialist pain management, is most effective.

what is the difference between natural f: Natural family planning (NFP, also called fertility awareness-based methods — FABMs) encompass several approaches to identifying fertile and infertile days of the menstrual cycle, enabling either conception avoidance or achievement. The Calendar (rhythm) method calculates the expected fertile window based on cycle length history — least reliable in those with irregular cycles (typical use failure rate approximately 9 to 25% per year). The Basal Body Temperature (BBT) method tracks the 0.2 to 0.4°C rise in waking temperature that occurs after ovulation due to progesterone — confirms ovulation has occurred (useful for retrospective identification but not prospective prediction). The Cervical Mucus (Billings Ovulation) Method identifies the fertile window by tracking changes in cervical secretions from dry/thick (infertile) through wet/transparent/stretchy (fertile) to thick again (infertile post-ovulation) — typical use failure rate approximately 3 to 15% per year. The Sympto-Thermal Method (STM) combines temperature and mucus monitoring — considered the most reliable NFP approach (perfect use failure rate approximately 0.4%; typical use approximately 1 to 2%). The Daysy and Natural Cycles devices are digital implementations of symptothermal-like algorithms.

what is the copper IUD as emergency cont: The copper intrauterine device (IUD) is the most effective form of emergency contraception available, with a failure rate of less than 0.1% when inserted within 5 days of unprotected sex. Its effectiveness does not decrease the longer within that 5-day window it is inserted (unlike the emergency contraceptive pill, which decreases in effectiveness with time). The copper IUD works by preventing fertilisation (copper ions are spermicidal and toxic to eggs) and preventing implantation. Advantages over emergency contraceptive pills: much higher efficacy; works regardless of body weight (emergency contraceptive pills are significantly less effective in women over 75 kg); and provides ongoing contraception for up to 5 to 10 years after insertion (converting the emergency intervention to ongoing long-acting contraception). It is particularly important to offer the copper IUD as the first-line recommendation for emergency contraception in women who weigh over 70 to 75 kg, where levonorgestrel pill efficacy is substantially reduced, and where ulipristal acetate (ellaOne) may also have reduced efficacy above 85 to 95 kg.

what is ulipristal acetate emergency con: Ulipristal acetate (ellaOne, 30 mg) is a selective progesterone receptor modulator used as emergency contraception, licensed for use up to 120 hours (5 days) after unprotected sex. It maintains high efficacy across the full 5-day window, whereas levonorgestrel EC (Levonelle, Plan B) decreases in efficacy after 72 hours. Ulipristal acetate works primarily by inhibiting or delaying ovulation — it is effective even when given immediately before ovulation (unlike levonorgestrel, which is less effective at this late point in the follicular phase). Important interaction: ulipristal acetate should not be taken within 5 days of starting hormonal contraception and hormonal contraception should not be started within 5 days after taking ulipristal — both contain progesterone agonists/antagonists and interaction reduces efficacy. In the UK, ulipristal acetate is available from pharmacies without prescription. Weight considerations: efficacy may be reduced in women over 85 to 95 kg (though less dramatically than levonorgestrel above 70 kg) — the copper IUD remains the most reliable option for larger women requiring emergency contraception.

what is the vasectomy and does it affect: A vasectomy is a surgical procedure for male sterilisation — the vas deferens (the tubes carrying sperm from the testes to the urethra) are cut, tied, sealed, or blocked under local anaesthetic. It has no effect on testosterone levels, sexual function, or ejaculation (only the small fraction of ejaculate volume contributed by sperm — approximately 2 to 5% — is absent). A vasectomy has absolutely no effect on the female partner's menstrual cycle, hormones, or health — it is a male surgical procedure. For female sterilisation, the fallopian tubes are occluded by laparoscopic surgery (Filshie clips, tubal ligation, or salpingectomy — removal of the tubes). Female sterilisation also does not affect the menstrual cycle because it does not alter ovarian function or hormone production — periods continue normally. Post-sterilisation menstrual changes that women notice reflect the cessation of hormonal contraception rather than the sterilisation procedure itself.

what are the contraceptive options for w: Migraines with aura (neurological visual, sensory, or speech symptoms preceding the headache) are an important contraindication to combined estrogen-containing contraception. Estrogen increases the risk of ischaemic stroke in women with aura, and UKMEC (UK Medical Eligibility Criteria) classifies combined oral contraceptive pill, combined patch, and combined vaginal ring as UKMEC 4 (absolute contraindication) in women with migraine with aura regardless of age. Safe contraceptive options in women with migraine with aura: all progesterone-only methods are safe — progesterone-only pill (POP), Nexplanon implant, Depo-Provera injection, levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena, Kyleena, Jaydess), and the non-hormonal copper IUD. Progesterone-only methods may improve migraines in some women (particularly those with menstrual migraine who benefit from stabilisation of estrogen levels). The levonorgestrel IUS is an excellent choice as it reduces menstrual blood loss (beneficial if menstrual migraine is triggered by menstruation), provides highly effective contraception, and is very low in systemic progesterone.

The WHO and UKMEC provide guidance on contraception during breastfeeding. Combined hormonal contraception (containing estrogen) is not recommended in the first 6 months of breastfeeding — estrogen reduces milk supply and is transferred to breast milk (though evidence of harm to the infant is limited, UKMEC 4 applies in the first 6 weeks postpartum and UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months). Safe contraceptive options while breastfeeding from any time postpartum: progesterone-only pill (POP) — does not affect milk supply; Nexplanon implant — can be inserted immediately postpartum; Depo-Provera injection — can be given immediately postpartum; levonorgestrel IUS — typically inserted from 4 weeks postpartum; copper IUD — can be inserted within 48 hours of delivery (immediate postplacental insertion) or from 4 weeks postpartum. Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM): exclusive breastfeeding (6 feeds per 24 hours including at night), fully amenorrhoeic, and baby under 6 months provides approximately 98% contraceptive protection — all three criteria must be met simultaneously.

what is the morning after pill and how e: The morning after pill (emergency contraceptive pill) is not one pill but two different medications with different mechanisms and efficacy. Levonorgestrel (Levonelle 1500, Plan B — available over the counter): a synthetic progesterone that delays or inhibits ovulation; taken as a single 1.5 mg dose ideally within 72 hours but licensed up to 120 hours; efficacy approximately 95% if taken within 24 hours, 85% within 24 to 48 hours, 58% within 48 to 72 hours; efficacy significantly reduced in women over 70 to 75 kg (halved or absent at 80 kg+ for some preparations). Ulipristal acetate (ellaOne — available over the counter in the UK): a selective progesterone receptor modulator; more effective than levonorgestrel across the 5-day window, particularly after 72 hours; can inhibit ovulation even when the LH surge is already underway. Both pills can cause nausea, headache, and irregular bleeding for the next cycle. They are not abortifacients — they act before fertilisation (levonorgestrel) or at latest before implantation (ulipristal). They do not terminate an established pregnancy and are not recommended if pregnancy is already confirmed.

what is permanent contraception (sterili: Female sterilisation (tubal occlusion or salpingectomy) is a permanent method of contraception for women who have completed their family. Methods: laparoscopic tubal occlusion using Filshie clips (applied across the fallopian tube, mechanically blocking it) under general anaesthetic as a day procedure; bilateral salpingectomy (complete surgical removal of the fallopian tubes — increasingly preferred over clips because it eliminates the small risk of ectopic pregnancy if clips fail and has been shown to reduce ovarian cancer risk by approximately 40 to 65%, since many high-grade serous ovarian cancers may originate in the fallopian tube fimbria). Failure rate of female sterilisation: approximately 1 in 200 over 10 years for clips (slightly higher in younger women). Failure of Filshie clips results in a higher proportion of ectopic pregnancies compared to the background rate — awareness of ectopic signs in the event of post-sterilisation pregnancy is essential. Female sterilisation does not affect menstrual cycles or hormone levels. Reversal is possible but not reliably successful and is not funded on the NHS — sterilisation should be considered permanent.

what is the subdermal contraceptive impl: The Nexplanon implant is a flexible plastic rod (4 cm long, 2 mm wide) inserted subdermally into the upper inner arm under local anaesthetic, lasting 3 years. It releases low-dose etonogestrel (a progestogen) continuously, preventing pregnancy primarily by suppressing ovulation (very reliable ovulation suppression), thickening cervical mucus (preventing sperm penetration), and thinning the endometrium. It is the most effective reversible contraceptive available (failure rate <0.1% — lower than sterilisation). Effects on menstrual bleeding: approximately 20% of users have no periods (amenorrhoea — often welcomed); 50% have infrequent, lighter, or irregular bleeding; 20% have frequent or prolonged light bleeding (the most common reason for early removal). Irregular bleeding typically improves by 3 to 6 months. Short courses of a combined pill or mefenamic acid can be prescribed alongside the implant to regulate breakthrough bleeding. The implant is immediately reversible (remove it and fertility returns rapidly); suitable for breastfeeding; and suitable for women with contraindications to estrogen. It does not cause weight gain in RCT evidence.

what is gender-based violence and its ef: Gender-based violence (GBV), including intimate partner violence (IPV), sexual violence, and female genital mutilation (FGM), has profound and well-documented effects on women's reproductive and menstrual health. Sexual violence causes acute and chronic genital trauma, STIs (including HIV), unwanted pregnancy, and long-term pelvic pain. IPV is strongly associated with: worsening dysmenorrhea and menstrual irregularity (through chronic stress axis activation and HPG axis disruption); higher rates of unintended pregnancy (reproductive coercion — partner controlling or sabotaging contraception); higher rates of STIs from forced unprotected sex; and delayed presentation to healthcare. PTSD and complex trauma (common in survivors of violence) dysregulate the HPA and HPG axes, causing hypothalamic amenorrhoea, menstrual irregularity, and amplified premenstrual symptoms. Healthcare providers should be trained in trauma-informed care and offer routine enquiry about domestic violence in reproductive health settings — NICE guidelines recommend routine enquiry in sexual and reproductive health services, antenatal clinics, and mental health services.

what is female genital mutilation (FGM): Female genital mutilation (FGM) encompasses all procedures involving partial or total removal of the external female genitalia or other injury to female genital organs for non-medical reasons. WHO classifies FGM into four types: Type I (clitoridectomy — partial or total removal of the clitoris and/or prepuce); Type II (excision — partial or total removal of clitoris and labia minora, with or without labia majora); Type III (infibulation — narrowing of the vaginal opening by cutting and repositioning the labia, sometimes with suturing — the most severe type, found predominantly in East Africa); Type IV (all other harmful procedures). FGM has no health benefits and causes severe short-term harm (bleeding, infection, pain, psychological trauma) and long-term complications (menstrual retention with infibulation, recurrent UTIs and vaginal infections, dyspareunia, sexual dysfunction, obstetric complications, PTSD, chronic pain). In the UK, FGM is illegal (Female Genital Mutilation Act 2003), and mandatory reporting applies for cases identified in under-18s. Deinfibulation (surgical opening of infibulation) is provided on the NHS to women with Type III FGM.

what is polycystic ovary syndrome and it: PCOS carries long-term health risks beyond its reproductive manifestations that require proactive monitoring and management. Metabolic risks: insulin resistance (present in approximately 65 to 70% of women with PCOS, regardless of weight) predisposes to type 2 diabetes — the lifetime risk of T2DM in PCOS is approximately 5 to 10 times higher than in unaffected women; impaired glucose tolerance is found in approximately 31 to 35% of women with PCOS by their mid-40s. Cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, dyslipidaemia, central obesity, insulin resistance) are clustered in PCOS, increasing long-term cardiovascular risk, though whether PCOS independently increases cardiovascular events (beyond its metabolic risk factors) remains debated. Endometrial cancer risk: chronic anovulation means no progesterone opposition of estrogen — the endometrium is under continuous estrogen stimulation, increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer (estimated 2 to 3-fold increased risk). Women with PCOS and oligomenorrhoea should have endometrial protection — with combined hormonal contraception or at minimum cyclical progestogens — and 5-yearly endometrial surveillance is considered in those with persistent amenorrhoea. Mental health: anxiety, depression, and body image concerns are 2 to 3 times more prevalent in PCOS.

what is the impact of weight on periods: Low body weight and low body fat have a profound effect on the menstrual cycle. The HPG axis is sensitive to energy availability signals — when calorie intake is insufficient to meet both metabolic and reproductive demands, the hypothalamus reduces GnRH pulsatility (by mechanisms including reduced leptin signalling from adipose tissue, elevated cortisol, and altered kisspeptin signalling). This produces hypothalamic amenorrhoea (HA) — absence of periods from HPG axis suppression. A body fat percentage below approximately 17 to 22% is associated with menstrual disruption in most women. Conditions associated with underweight-related amenorrhoea: anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa (particularly restrictive subtypes), relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S), and extreme low-calorie dieting. Consequences of prolonged hypothalamic amenorrhoea: hypoestrogenaemia causes bone density loss at the rate of approximately 2 to 5% per year — young women can develop osteoporosis at an age when they should be building bone. Estrogen replacement (combined pill or transdermal HRT) is used alongside nutritional rehabilitation. Resolution of HA requires restoring adequate energy availability — periods typically resume within 3 to 6 months of returning to a healthy weight and adequate caloric intake.

what are phytoestrogens and how do they: Phytoestrogens are plant-derived compounds with weak estrogenic activity — they bind to estrogen receptors but with much lower potency than endogenous estradiol (1000 to 10,000 times weaker). Major classes: isoflavones (soy, red clover — genistein, daidzein); lignans (flaxseeds, sesame seeds — converted by gut bacteria to enterolactone and enterodiol); coumestans (clover, alfalfa). In populations with high soy intake (Japan, China), phytoestrogens may modestly lengthen the menstrual cycle (by delaying the LH surge) and may reduce menopausal vasomotor symptoms — evidence from RCTs is mixed, with effect sizes smaller than pharmaceutical HRT. For menopausal symptoms, isoflavone supplements (40 to 80 mg genistein daily) have shown modest benefit in RCTs for hot flush reduction. In women with estrogen-sensitive conditions (endometriosis, breast cancer), the clinical significance of dietary phytoestrogens is debated — at typical dietary intakes, they are unlikely to cause harm and may be mildly beneficial, but high-dose isolated isoflavone supplements in women with estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer are of uncertain safety and are not generally recommended without specialist guidance.

what is hyperthyroidism symptoms and men: Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid — elevated T3/T4 with suppressed TSH) has characteristic menstrual effects. The most common causes are Graves' disease (autoimmune — stimulating TSH receptor antibodies), toxic multinodular goitre, and toxic adenoma. Menstrual manifestations: oligomenorrhoea (infrequent periods), hypomenorrhoea (light periods), and in some cases amenorrhoea — excess thyroid hormone increases SHBG (reducing free estrogen), disrupts GnRH pulsatility, and increases estrogen metabolism. Systemic symptoms: heat intolerance, sweating, palpitations (atrial fibrillation in severe cases), weight loss despite good appetite, tremor, anxiety, diarrhoea, and exophthalmos (in Graves' disease). Treatment: antithyroid drugs (carbimazole or propylthiouracil — PTU preferred in pregnancy), radioactive iodine (absolute contraindication in pregnancy; contraception required for 6 months after), or surgical thyroidectomy. Carbimazole is teratogenic (associated with aplasia cutis and choanal atresia); if antithyroid drugs are needed in the first trimester, PTU is preferred. Once euthyroid, menstrual cycles normalise.

what is gestational diabetes and how doe: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is glucose intolerance developing or first diagnosed in pregnancy, affecting approximately 4 to 5% of pregnancies in the UK (higher in South Asian, Black African and Caribbean, and Middle Eastern ethnic groups). It occurs because pregnancy hormones (particularly human placental lactogen) cause insulin resistance that the pancreas cannot fully compensate for in predisposed individuals. GDM increases the risk of pre-eclampsia, caesarean section, macrosomia (large baby — causing birth complications), neonatal hypoglycaemia, and stillbirth. Postpartum: GDM typically resolves after delivery, and glucose tolerance should be reassessed at 6 to 13 weeks postpartum (HbA1c or fasting glucose). Women with GDM have a 35 to 50% lifetime risk of developing type 2 diabetes — ongoing postpartum surveillance and lifestyle modification are essential. Postpartum cycles: GDM per se does not directly cause menstrual cycle disruption beyond the general hormonal changes of the postpartum period; however, if GDM reflects an underlying predisposition to insulin resistance (as in PCOS), irregular cycles may have predated pregnancy and may recur postpartum.

what does it mean if my period is late b: A late period with a negative pregnancy test is common and has many causes. Before concluding a cycle is 'late', it's important to know your usual cycle length — normal cycles range from 24 to 38 days, so a cycle that exceeds your personal average by a week or more is genuinely late.
